   663→   663	
   664→   664	Title "Dropping the Last Element"
   665→   665	
   666→   666	Introduction "
   667→   667	# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail
   668→   668	
   669→   669	You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
   670→   670	its dual:
   671→   671	
   672→   672	**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.
   673→   673	
   674→   674	Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
   675→   675	tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:
   676→   676	
   677→   677	$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$
   678→   678	
   679→   679	The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
   680→   680	that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.
   681→   681	
   682→   682	The duality:
   683→   683	- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
   684→   684	- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`
   685→   685	
   686→   686	**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
   687→   687	"
   688→   688	
   689→   689	/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
   690→   690	Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
   691→   691	    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
   692→   692	    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
   693→   693	  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   694→   694	  constructor
   695→   695	  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
   696→   696	    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
   697→   697	    rfl
   698→   698	  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
   699→   699	    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
   700→   700	    rfl
   701→   701	
   702→   702	Conclusion "
   703→   703	`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:
   704→   704	
   705→   705	| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
   706→   706	|---|---|---|---|
   707→   707	| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
   708→   708	| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |
   709→   709	
   710→   710	Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
   711→   711	- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
   712→   712	- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)
   713→   713	
   714→   714	In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
   715→   715	`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
   716→   716	"
   717→   717	
   718→   718	/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.
   719→   719	
   720→   720	## Syntax
   721→   721	```
   722→   722	Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
   723→   723	```
   724→   724	
   725→   725	## Type
   726→   726	`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`
   727→   727	
   728→   728	## Definition
   729→   729	`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`
   730→   730	
   731→   731	## Example
   732→   732	`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.
   733→   733	
   734→   734	## Key identity (taught in the next level)
   735→   735	`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
   736→   736	its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
   737→   737	-/
   738→   738	DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"
   739→   739	
   740→   740	NewDefinition Fin.init
   741→   741

   744→   744	
   745→   745	World "FinTuples"
   746→   746	Level 11
   747→   747	
   748→   748	Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"
   749→   749	
   750→   750	Introduction "
   751→   751	# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last
   752→   752	
   753→   753	You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:
   754→   754	
   755→   755	$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$
   756→   756	
   757→   757	The dual identity is:
   758→   758	
   759→   759	$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$
   760→   760	
   761→   761	In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
   762→   762	appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.
   763→   763	
   764→   764	The cons/snoc duality is now complete:
   765→   765	
   766→   766	| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
   767→   767	|---|---|---|
   768→   768	| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
   769→   769	| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |
   770→   770	
   771→   771	**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
   772→   772	"
   773→   773	
   774→   774	/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
   775→   775	Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
   776→   776	    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
   777→   777	  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
   778→   778	  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   779→   779	  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
   780→   780	  exact vecSnoc_self_init f
   781→   781	
   782→   782	Conclusion "
   783→   783	The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
   784→   784	decomposed from either end:
   785→   785	
   786→   786	**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
   787→   787	**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`
   788→   788	
   789→   789	Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
   790→   790	reassembling always recovers the original tuple.
   791→   791	
   792→   792	This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
   793→   793	can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
   794→   794	whole sequence either way.
   795→   795	"
   796→   796

   804→   804	
   805→   805	Title "ext for Functions"
   806→   806	
   807→   807	Introduction "
   808→   808	# Extensionality for Functions
   809→   809	
   810→   810	In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
   811→   811	equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
   812→   812	use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.
   813→   813	
   814→   814	For functions, extensionality says:
   815→   815	
   816→   816	> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.
   817→   817	
   818→   818	In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.
   819→   819	
   820→   820	When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
   821→   821	`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.
   822→   822	
   823→   823	This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
   824→   824	In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
   825→   825	Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.
   826→   826	
   827→   827	**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
   828→   828	"
   829→   829	
   830→   830	/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
   831→   831	Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
   832→   832	    f = g := by
   833→   833	  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
   834→   834	  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
   835→   835	  ext i
   836→   836	  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
   837→   837	  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
   838→   838	  exact h i
   839→   839	
   840→   840	Conclusion "
   841→   841	The pattern is simple:
   842→   842	
   843→   843	1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
   844→   844	2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
   845→   845	
   846→   846	This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
   847→   847	Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
   848→   848	are equal when their components are equal.
   849→   849	
   850→   850	Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
   851→   851	for functions. Both are valid.
   852→   852	"
   853→   853	
   854→   854	/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
   855→   855	
   856→   856	## For Fin elements
   857→   857	When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
   858→   858	`a.val = b.val` — two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
   859→   859	natural numbers are equal.
   860→   860	
   861→   861	## For functions
   862→   862	When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : α → β`, `ext i` introduces
   863→   863	a variable `i : α` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` — two
   864→   864	functions are equal when they agree on every input.
   865→   865	
   866→   866	## For finsets
   867→   867	When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset α`, `ext x` introduces
   868→   868	a variable `x : α` and reduces the goal to `x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ t` — two
   869→   869	finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
   870→   870	
   871→   871	All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
   872→   872	equal when their components are equal.
   873→   873	-/
   874→   874	TacticDoc ext
   875→   875

   880→   880	World "FinTuples"
   881→   881	Level 13
   882→   882	
   883→   883	Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"
   884→   884	
   885→   885	Introduction "
   886→   886	# Transforming Tuples
   887→   887	
   888→   888	A tuple `f : Fin 2 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
   889→   889	A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
   890→   890	`g circ f : Fin 2 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
   891→   891	to each entry.
   892→   892	
   893→   893	For example, if `f = ![1, 2]` and `g` doubles its input,
   894→   894	then `g circ f = ![2, 4]` — applying `g` to each entry.
   895→   895	
   896→   896	This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
   897→   897	`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
   898→   898	element of a finite set.
   899→   899	
   900→   900	**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
   901→   901	both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
   902→   902	"
   903→   903	
   904→   904	/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
   905→   905	Statement (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (g : ℕ → ℕ)
   906→   906	    (hf : f = ![1, 2]) (hg : ∀ x, g x = x * 2) :
   907→   907	    g ∘ f = ![2, 4] := by
   908→   908	  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to reduce to pointwise equality."
   909→   909	  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   910→   910	  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ⟨v, hv⟩` on the left — the
   911→   911	  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
   912→   912	  to unfold this to `g (f ⟨v, hv⟩)`, then substitute both
   913→   913	  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
   914→   914	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
   915→   915	  a single rewrite chain."
   916→   916	  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
   917→   917	  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
   918→   918	  cases v with
   919→   919	  | zero => rfl
   920→   920	  | succ n =>
   921→   921	    cases n with
   922→   922	    | zero => rfl
   923→   923	    | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   924→   924	
   925→   925	Conclusion "
   926→   926	Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
   927→   927	`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1)]`.
   928→   928	
   929→   929	The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
   930→   930	an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
   931→   931	`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.
   932→   932	
   933→   933	**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
   934→   934	function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
   935→   935	composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
   936→   936	Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
   937→   937	transformations.
   938→   938	"
   939→   939	
   940→   940	NewTheorem Function.comp_apply
   941→   941

   946→   946	World "FinTuples"
   947→   947	Level 14
   948→   948	
   949→   949	Title "Tuples as Data"
   950→   950	
   951→   951	Introduction "
   952→   952	# Tuples Represent Real Data
   953→   953	
   954→   954	So far, every exercise has been about structural properties of
   955→   955	tuples: how `cons` and `tail` relate, how `ext` proves equality.
   956→   956	Now you'll see tuples used as **data** — specifically, as vectors
   957→   957	whose entries you can compute with.
   958→   958	
   959→   959	The tuple `![1, 0]` can represent a 2D vector. The tuple
   960→   960	`![2, 3]` is another. Their **componentwise sum** — adding
   961→   961	corresponding entries — should give `![3, 3]`:
   962→   962

500	
   501	But a true inverse relationship goes both ways. The reverse
   502	direction asks: if you START with a head `a` and a tail `g`,
   503	cons them together, and then decompose — do you get back `a`
   504	and `g`?
   505	
   506	Concretely:
   507	- `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` — the head of the assembled tuple is `a`
   508	- `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` — the tail of the assembled tuple is `g`
   509	
   510	Together with Level 7, this shows that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)`
   511	are genuine inverse operations — a bijection between
   512	`α × (Fin n → α)` and `Fin (n + 1) → α`.
   513	
   514	**Your task**: Prove both round-trip properties. Use `rw [hg]`
   515	to substitute the hypothesis, then the access lemma or `ext`.
   516	"
   517	
   518	/-- Assembling and decomposing recovers the original components. -/
   519	Statement (a : ℕ) (g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : Fin 3 → ℕ) (hh : h = Fin.cons a g) :
   520	    h 0 = a ∧ Fin.tail h = g := by
   521	  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   522	  constructor
   523	  · Hint "The goal is `h 0 = a`. Rewrite `h` using `hh`, then
   524	    simplify: `rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]`."
   525	    rw [hh, Fin.cons_val_zero]
   526	  · Hint "The goal is `Fin.tail h = g`. After `rw [hh]`, the
   527	    goal becomes `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g`.
   528	    Use `ext i` to check pointwise, then `rfl`."
   529	    rw [hh]
   530	    Hint "Now use `ext i` to reduce function equality to
   531	    pointwise equality."
   532	    ext i
   533	    Hint "The goal `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) i = g i` holds
   534	    because `Fin.tail` evaluates at `i.succ`, and
   535	    `Fin.cons a g i.succ = g i` by definition. Use `rfl`."
   536	    rfl
   537	
   538	Conclusion "
   539	The round-trip is now complete. `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` are
   540	genuine inverses:
   541	
   542	| Direction | Identity |
   543	|---|---|
   544	| Decompose then reassemble | `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` |
   545	| Assemble then decompose (head) | `(Fin.cons a g) 0 = a` |
   546	| Assemble then decompose (tail) | `Fin.tail (Fin.cons a g) = g` |
   547	
   548	This means `Fin (n + 1) → α` is in bijection with
   549	`α × (Fin n → α)` — an `(n+1)`-tuple is exactly a head
   550	element plus an `n`-tuple. This decomposition is the foundation
   551	for reasoning about tuples by induction on length.
   552	
   553	Next, you'll learn the dual construction: appending an element
   554	at the *end* of a tuple using `vecSnoc`.
   555	"

   560	World "FinTuples"
   561	Level 9
   562	
   563	Title "Appending with vecSnoc"
   564	
   565	Introduction "
   566	# vecSnoc: Adding to the End
   567	
   568	`Fin.cons` prepends an element at the *beginning* of a tuple.
   569	What about adding an element at the *end*?
   570	
   571	**`vecSnoc f x`** takes:
   572	- A tuple `f : Fin n → α`
   573	- An element `x : α`
   574	
   575	and produces `vecSnoc f x : Fin (n + 1) → α` — the tuple `f`
   576	with `x` appended at the end.
   577	
   578	The access lemmas are:
   579	- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — the last element is `x`
   580	- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — earlier elements are from `f`
   581	
   582	Notice the connection to FinNavigation: `Fin.cons` routes through
   583	`0` / `succ` (the successor decomposition), while `vecSnoc` routes
   584	through `castSucc` / `last` (the other decomposition you learned).
   585	These are **dual constructions** — one builds from the front,
   586	the other from the back.
   587	
   588	**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas for `vecSnoc`.
   589	"
   590	
   591	/-- The access lemmas for vecSnoc. -/
   592	Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
   593	    vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a ∧
   594	    ∀ i : Fin 2, vecSnoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
   595	  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   596	  constructor
   597	  · Hint "The goal is `vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a`.
   598	    Use `exact vecSnoc_last f a`."
   599	    exact vecSnoc_last f a
   600	  · Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use
   601	    `exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i`."
   602	    intro i
   603	    exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i
   604	
   605	Conclusion "
   606	You now have two ways to build tuples:
   607	
   608	| Operation | Adds where | Routes through |
   609	|---|---|---|
   610	| `Fin.cons a f` | Beginning | `0` / `succ` |
   611	| `vecSnoc f x` | End | `castSucc` / `last` |
   612	
   613	The duality is exact:
   614	- `Fin.cons` uses the **0/succ decomposition** from FinNavigation:
   615	  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `0` or `i.succ`
   616	- `vecSnoc` uses the **castSucc/last decomposition**:
   617	  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `i.castSucc` or `Fin.last n`
   618	
   619	This is the payoff from learning both decompositions in
   620	FinNavigation — they power the two dual tuple constructors.
   621	"
   622	
   623	/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
   624	producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.
   625	
   626	## Syntax
   627	```
   628	vecSnoc f x    -- append x to the end of f
   629	```
   630	
   631	## Type
   632	`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`
   633	
   634	## Element access
   635	- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — `vecSnoc_last`
   636	- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — `vecSnoc_castSucc`
   637	
   638	## Example
   639	`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
   640	-/
   641	DefinitionDoc vecSnoc as "vecSnoc"
   642	
   643	/-- `vecSnoc_last f x` states that `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x`.
   644	
   645	The last element of a snoc'd tuple is the appended element.
   646	-/
   647	TheoremDoc vecSnoc_last as "vecSnoc_last" in "Fin"
   648	
   649	/-- `vecSnoc_castSucc f x i` states that `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i`.
   650	
   651	The earlier elements of a snoc'd tuple come from the original tuple.
   652	-/
   653	TheoremDoc vecSnoc_castSucc as "vecSnoc_castSucc" in "Fin"

   655	NewDefinition vecSnoc
   656	NewTheorem vecSnoc_last vecSnoc_castSucc

   661	World "FinTuples"
   662	Level 10
   663	
   664	Title "Dropping the Last Element"
   665	
   666	Introduction "
   667	# Fin.init: The Dual of Fin.tail
   668	
   669	You've seen `Fin.tail`, which drops the **first** element. Now meet
   670	its dual:
   671	
   672	**`Fin.init`** drops the **last** element.
   673	
   674	Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.init f` is a shorter
   675	tuple `Fin n → α` that keeps all elements except the last:
   676	
   677	$$\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i.\\texttt{castSucc})$$
   678	
   679	The definition uses `castSucc` — exactly the FinNavigation operation
   680	that embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` without reaching the last index.
   681	
   682	The duality:
   683	- `Fin.tail` drops the first: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`
   684	- `Fin.init` drops the last: `Fin.init ![a, b, c]` has values `a, b`
   685	
   686	**Your task**: Verify the values of `Fin.init ![a, b, c]`.
   687	"
   688	
   689	/-- `Fin.init` keeps all elements except the last. -/
   690	Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
   691	    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 0 = a ∧
   692	    (Fin.init (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ)) 1 = b := by
   693	  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   694	  constructor
   695	  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 0 is `![a, b, c]` at
   696	    `0.castSucc = 0`, which is `a`. Use `rfl`."
   697	    rfl
   698	  · Hint "`Fin.init ![a, b, c]` at index 1 is `![a, b, c]` at
   699	    `1.castSucc = 1`, which is `b`. Use `rfl`."
   700	    rfl
   701	
   702	Conclusion "
   703	`Fin.init` and `Fin.tail` are complementary:
   704	
   705	| Operation | Drops | Keeps | Routes through |
   706	|---|---|---|---|
   707	| `Fin.tail f` | First (`f 0`) | `f 1, f 2, ..., f n` | `succ` |
   708	| `Fin.init f` | Last (`f (last n)`) | `f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)` | `castSucc` |
   709	
   710	Notice how the FinNavigation decompositions appear again:
   711	- `Fin.tail` uses **succ** (the `0`/`succ` decomposition)
   712	- `Fin.init` uses **castSucc** (the `castSucc`/`last` decomposition)
   713	
   714	In the next level, you'll see the reconstruction identity for
   715	`Fin.init` and `vecSnoc` — the dual of `Fin.cons_self_tail`.
   716	"
   717	
   718	/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.
   719	
   720	## Syntax
   721	```
   722	Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
   723	```
   724	
   725	## Type
   726	`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`
   727	
   728	## Definition
   729	`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`
   730	
   731	## Example
   732	`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.
   733	
   734	## Key identity (taught in the next level)
   735	`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` — every tuple equals
   736	its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
   737	-/
   738	DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"

   745	World "FinTuples"
   746	Level 11
   747	
   748	Title "Init-Snoc Reconstruction"
   749	
   750	Introduction "
   751	# Every Tuple = Init Snoc'd with Last
   752	
   753	You know that every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail:
   754	
   755	$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f \\quad (\\texttt{Fin.cons\\_self\\_tail})$$
   756	
   757	The dual identity is:
   758	
   759	$$\\texttt{vecSnoc}\\;(\\texttt{Fin.init}\\;f)\\;(f\\;(\\texttt{Fin.last}\\;n)) = f \\quad (\\texttt{vecSnoc\\_self\\_init})$$
   760	
   761	In words: taking all elements except the last (init), then
   762	appending the last element (snoc), recovers the original tuple.
   763	
   764	The cons/snoc duality is now complete:
   765	
   766	| Build from | Decompose into | Reconstruct with |
   767	|---|---|---|
   768	| Front (`Fin.cons`) | head (`f 0`) + tail (`Fin.tail f`) | `Fin.cons_self_tail` |
   769	| Back (`vecSnoc`) | init (`Fin.init f`) + last (`f (Fin.last n)`) | `vecSnoc_self_init` |
   770	
   771	**Your task**: Prove the init-snoc reconstruction identity.
   772	"
   773	
   774	/-- Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element. -/
   775	Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
   776	    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last 2)) = f := by
   777	  Hint "The theorem `vecSnoc_self_init f` states exactly this:
   778	  `vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   779	  Use `exact vecSnoc_self_init f`."
   780	  exact vecSnoc_self_init f
   781	
   782	Conclusion "
   783	The cons/snoc duality is now complete. Every tuple can be
   784	decomposed from either end:
   785	
   786	**From the front**: `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
   787	**From the back**: `f = vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n))`
   788	
   789	Both decompositions are inverse operations: decomposing and
   790	reassembling always recovers the original tuple.
   791	
   792	This is the tuple analog of a bidirectional linked list — you
   793	can traverse from the front or the back and reconstruct the
   794	whole sequence either way.
   795	"
   796	
   797	NewTheorem vecSnoc_self_init

   802	World "FinTuples"
   803	Level 12
   804	
   805	Title "ext for Functions"
   806	
   807	Introduction "
   808	# Extensionality for Functions
   809	
   810	In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
   811	equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
   812	use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.
   813	
   814	For functions, extensionality says:
   815	
   816	> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.
   817	
   818	In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.
   819	
   820	When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
   821	`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.
   822	
   823	This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
   824	In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
   825	Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.
   826	
   827	**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
   828	"
   829	
   830	/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
   831	Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
   832	    f = g := by
   833	  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
   834	  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
   835	  ext i
   836	  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
   837	  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
   838	  exact h i
   839	
   840	Conclusion "
   841	The pattern is simple:
   842	
   843	1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
   844	2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
   845	
   846	This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
   847	Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
   848	are equal when their components are equal.
   849	
   850	Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
   851	for functions. Both are valid.
   852	"
   853	
   854	/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
   855	
   856	## For Fin elements
   857	When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
   858	`a.val = b.val` — two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
   859	natural numbers are equal.
   860	
   861	## For functions
   862	When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : α → β`, `ext i` introduces
   863	a variable `i : α` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` — two
   864	functions are equal when they agree on every input.
   865	
   866	## For finsets
   867	When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset α`, `ext x` introduces
   868	a variable `x : α` and reduces the goal to `x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ t` — two
   869	finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
   870	
   871	All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
   872	equal when their components are equal.
   873	-/
   874	TacticDoc ext

   880	World "FinTuples"
   881	Level 13
   882	
   883	Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"
   884	
   885	Introduction "
   886	# Transforming Tuples
   887	
   888	A tuple `f : Fin 2 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
   889	A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
   890	`g circ f : Fin 2 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
   891	to each entry.
   892	
   893	For example, if `f = ![1, 2]` and `g` doubles its input,
   894	then `g circ f = ![2, 4]` — applying `g` to each entry.
   895	
   896	This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
   897	`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
   898	element of a finite set.
   899	
   900	**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
   901	both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
   902	"
   903	
   904	/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
   905	Statement (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (g : ℕ → ℕ)
   906	    (hf : f = ![1, 2]) (hg : ∀ x, g x = x * 2) :
   907	    g ∘ f = ![2, 4] := by
   908	  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to reduce to pointwise equality."
   909	  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
   910	  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ⟨v, hv⟩` on the left — the
   911	  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
   912	  to unfold this to `g (f ⟨v, hv⟩)`, then substitute both
   913	  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
   914	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
   915	  a single rewrite chain."
   916	  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
   917	  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
   918	  cases v with
   919	  | zero => rfl
   920	  | succ n =>
   921	    cases n with
   922	    | zero => rfl
   923	    | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   924	
   925	Conclusion "
   926	Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
   927	`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1)]`.
   928	
   929	The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
   930	an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
   931	`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.
   932	
   933	**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
   934	function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
   935	composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
   936	Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
   937	transformations.
   938	"
   939	
   940	NewTheorem Function.comp_apply

   946	World "FinTuples"
   947	Level 14
   948	
   949	Title "Tuples as Data"
   950	
   951	Introduction "
   952	# Tuples Represent Real Data
   953	
   954	So far, every exercise has been about structural properties of
   955	tuples: how `cons` and `tail` relate, how `ext` proves equality.
   956	Now you'll see tuples used as **data** — specifically, as vectors
   957	whose entries you can compute with.
   958	
   959	The tuple `![1, 0]` can represent a 2D vector. The tuple
   960	`![2, 3]` is another. Their **componentwise sum** — adding
   961	corresponding entries — should give `![3, 3]`:
   962	
   963	| Index | `![1, 0]` | `![2, 3]` | Sum |
   964	|---|---|---|---|
   965	| 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
   966	| 1 | 0 | 3 | 3 |
   967	
   968	In Lean, componentwise sum is expressed as a function:
   969	`fun i => v i + w i` — at each index `i`, add the entries.
   970	
   971	**Reading the goal**: The statement looks dense because it spells out
   972	the type annotations: `(![1, 0] : Fin 2 -> NN)`. Read it as:
   973	'the function that maps index `i` to `v[i] + w[i]` equals `![3, 3]`.'
   974	The type annotations are just Lean being explicit about the types.
   975	
   976	**Your task**: Prove that the componentwise sum of `![1, 0]`
   977	and `![2, 3]` equals `![3, 3]`.
   978	
   979	**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, then
   980	case-split on the index and verify each entry with `rfl`.
   981	"
   982	
   983	/-- Componentwise addition of two concrete vectors. -/

   508→   508	World "PsetFin"
   509→   509	Level 10
   510→   510	
   511→   511	Title "Modular Equality"
   512→   512	
   513→   513	Introduction "
   514→   514	# When Different Formulas Agree
   515→   515	
   516→   516	Two functions on `Fin 5` are described by different-looking formulas:
   517→   517	- `f i = i + 3`
   518→   518	- `g i = i + 8`
   519→   519	
   520→   520	Are they the same function? In ordinary arithmetic, adding 3 and
   521→   521	adding 8 give different results. But in `Fin 5`, the arithmetic wraps
   522→   522	around: `8 mod 5 = 3`, so `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. This means
   523→   523	`i + 8 = i + 3` for every `i : Fin 5`.
   524→   524	
   525→   525	**Your task**: Prove `f = g` using `ext` and the formula rewrites.
   526→   526	"
   527→   527	
   528→   528	/-- Two functions with the same modular formula are equal. -/
   529→   529	Statement (f g : Fin 5 → Fin 5)
   530→   530	    (hf : ∀ i : Fin 5, f i = i + 3)
   531→   531	    (hg : ∀ i : Fin 5, g i = i + 8) : f = g := by
   532→   532	  Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
   533→   533	  Use `ext i` to introduce the index."
   534→   534	  ext i
   535→   535	  Hint "Substitute both formulas: `rw [hf, hg]`.
   536→   536	  The resulting equality holds because `(3 : Fin 5) = (8 : Fin 5)`
   537→   537	  by modular arithmetic."
   538→   538	  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal becomes
   539→   539	  `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. Since `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`
   540→   540	  by modular arithmetic, `rfl` closes it."
   541→   541	  rw [hf, hg]
   542→   542	  Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides are definitionally equal because
   543→   543	  `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. Use `rfl`."
   544→   544	  rfl
   545→   545	
   546→   546	Conclusion "
   547→   547	After rewriting, the goal was `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. This is
   548→   548	true by `rfl` because `(8 : Fin 5)` and `(3 : Fin 5)` are the
   549→   549	**same element** — `8 mod 5 = 3`.
   550→   550	
   551→   551	This combines three skills:
   552→   552	- **Function extensionality** (`ext`) from FinTuples
   553→   553	- **Universally quantified rewrite** (`rw [hf, hg]`) from FinTuples
   554→   554	- **Modular arithmetic** from MeetFin Level 19
   555→   555	
   556→   556	**Why does `rfl` close `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`?** The numerals
   557→   557	`3` and `8` in `Fin 5` are *the same element*: both reduce to the
   558→   558	element with value `3` (since `8 mod 5 = 3`). Lean computes this
   559→   559	reduction during elaboration, so the two sides are definitionally
   560→   560	equal — no proof step required. This is the power of modular
   561→   561	arithmetic being built into the type: Lean *knows* that `(8 : Fin 5)`
   562→   562	and `(3 : Fin 5)` are identical.
   563→   563	
   564→   564	In a pset, recognizing which skills to combine is the real challenge.
   565→   565	"
   566→   566	
   567→   567	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
   568→   568	DisabledTheorem funext
   569→   569	import Game.Metadata
   570→   570	
   571→   571	World "PsetFin"
   572→   572	Level 11
   573→   573	
   574→   574	Title "The General Decomposition"
   575→   575	
   576→   576	Introduction "
   577→   577	# From Concrete to Abstract
   578→   578	
   579→   579	In the FinNavigation boss, you proved that every element of
   580→   580	`Fin 3` is either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   581→   581	That proof used exhaustive case analysis on all three values.
   582→   582	
   583→   583	Here you'll prove the **general** version: for ANY `n`, every
   584→   584	element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc`.
   585→   585	
   586→   586	The proof uses a different strategy. Instead of checking each
   587→   587	value, you split on whether `i.val = n` or `i.val < n`:
   588→   588	- If `i.val = n`: then `i = Fin.last n` (by `ext` + `Fin.val_last`)
   589→   589	- If `i.val < n`: then `i = j.castSucc` where `j = (i.val, proof)` (the L13 pattern from FinNavigation)
   590→   590	
   591→   591	The key insight: `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) means either
   592→   592	`i.val < n` or `i.val = n`. You can derive this disjunction
   593→   593	with `omega` and then case-split on it.
   594→   594	"
   595→   595	
   596→   596	/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. -/
   597→   597	Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = Fin.last n ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
   598→   598	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   599→   599	  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   600→   600	  intro i
   601→   601	  cases i with
   602→   602	  | mk v hv =>
   603→   603	    Hint "Now `v < n + 1`. This means either `v < n` or `v = n`.
   604→   604	    Create this disjunction: `have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`."
   605→   605	    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`"
   606→   606	    have h : v < n ∨ v = n := by omega
   607→   607	    Hint "Case-split on `h`: `cases h with | inl hlt => ... | inr heq => ...`"
   608→   608	    cases h with
   609→   609	    | inl hlt =>
   610→   610	      Hint "Here `v < n`, so the element is a `castSucc` image.
   611→   611	      Choose `right` and provide the witness."
   612→   612	      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨v, hlt⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
   613→   613	      right
   614→   614	      use ⟨v, hlt⟩
   615→   615	      ext
   616→   616	      rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   617→   617	    | inr heq =>
   618→   618	      Hint "Here `v = n`, so the element IS `Fin.last n`.
   619→   619	      Choose `left` and prove the equality."
   620→   620	      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
   621→   621	      left
   622→   622	      ext
   623→   623	      rw [Fin.val_last]
   624→   624	      exact heq
   625→   625	
   626→   626	Conclusion "
   627→   627	You've generalized the concrete decomposition to ALL `n`.
   628→   628	
   629→   629	The concrete proof (FinNavigation boss) worked by checking every
   630→   630	value of `Fin 3`. The abstract proof works by splitting on
   631→   631	`v < n or v = n` — a fact `omega` can derive from `v < n + 1`.
   632→   632	
   633→   633	Notice the **value repackaging** pattern (from FinNavigation L13):
   634→   634	`use ⟨v, hlt⟩` takes the same value `v` and justifies it in
   635→   635	`Fin n` with the new bound `hlt : v < n`. The value doesn't
   636→   636	change — only the type's upper bound shrinks.
   637→   637	
   638→   638	This is the first taste of **generalizing from concrete to
   639→   639	abstract**: the same structural fact holds, but the proof strategy
   640→   640	changes from exhaustive enumeration to case analysis on a
   641→   641	disjunction. This pattern appears throughout mathematics — concrete
   642→   642	examples reveal the structure, but general proofs use different
   643→   643	(often cleaner) strategies.
   644→   644	
   645→   645	(In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.lastCases` —
   646→   646	an eliminator that lets you handle the last element and castSucc
   647→   647	images separately. You just proved the underlying fact by hand.)
   648→   648	"
   649→   649

   653→   653	
   654→   654	World "PsetFin"
   655→   655	Level 12
   656→   656	
   657→   657	Title "The Dual Decomposition"
   658→   658	
   659→   659	Introduction "
   660→   660	# General 0/succ Decomposition
   661→   661	
   662→   662	In Level 11, you proved the general **castSucc/last decomposition**:
   663→   663	every element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
   664→   664	`j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   665→   665	
   666→   666	The course has emphasized that castSucc/last and 0/succ are **dual**
   667→   667	decompositions — mirror images of each other. But so far, you've
   668→   668	only proved the 0/succ decomposition concretely for `Fin 3`
   669→   669	(FinNavigation Level 10). Here you'll prove it for general `n`.
   670→   670	
   671→   671	The proof mirrors L11's structure: instead of splitting on
   672→   672	`v = n` vs `v < n`, you split on `v = 0` vs `v >= 1`.
   673→   673	- If `v = 0`: the element is `0`
   674→   674	- If `v >= 1`: the element is `j.succ` where `j = (v - 1, proof)`
   675→   675	
   676→   676	**Your task**: Prove the general 0/succ decomposition.
   677→   677	"
   678→   678	
   679→   679	/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either 0 or a successor. -/
   680→   680	Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.succ := by
   681→   681	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   682→   682	  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   683→   683	  intro i
   684→   684	  cases i with
   685→   685	  | mk v hv =>
   686→   686	    Hint "Now case-split on `v` itself: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
   687→   687	    cases v with
   688→   688	    | zero =>
   689→   689	      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
   690→   690	      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; rfl`"
   691→   691	      left
   692→   692	      rfl
   693→   693	    | succ m =>
   694→   694	      Hint "Here `v = m + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where `j = ⟨m, ...⟩ : Fin n`.
   695→   695	      Choose `right`, provide the witness, then prove via `ext` + `Fin.val_succ`."
   696→   696	      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨m, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_succ]`"
   697→   697	      right
   698→   698	      use ⟨m, by omega⟩
   699→   699	      ext
   700→   700	      rw [Fin.val_succ]
   701→   701	
   702→   702	Conclusion "
   703→   703	You've completed the **dual** of Level 11:
   704→   704	
   705→   705	| Decomposition | General proof | Splits on |
   706→   706	|---|---|---|
   707→   707	| castSucc/last (L11) | `v = n` or `v < n` | Top boundary |
   708→   708	| 0/succ (this level) | `v = 0` or `v >= 1` | Bottom boundary |
   709→   709	
   710→   710	The **value repackaging** pattern appeared again: `use ⟨m, by omega⟩`
   711→   711	takes the predecessor value and repackages it into `Fin n`.
   712→   712	
   713→   713	Both decompositions are now proved for arbitrary `n`. This
   714→   714	symmetry is fundamental: it's why tuples can be built from
   715→   715	*either end* — `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ` (prepend),
   716→   716	`vecSnoc` routes through `castSucc`/`last` (append).
   717→   717	
   718→   718	In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.cases` —
   719→   719	the 0/succ dual of `Fin.lastCases`.
   720→   720	
   721→   721	There's a unifying concept behind both decompositions:
   722→   722	`Fin.succAbove p` is an embedding `Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that
   723→   723	'skips' position `p`. When `p = Fin.last n`, `succAbove` is
   724→   724	`castSucc` (skip the last position). When `p = 0`, `succAbove`
   725→   725	is `succ` (skip position 0). Both `castSucc` and `succ` are
   726→   726	special cases of the same 'skip one position' operation. This
   727→   727	means the castSucc/last and 0/succ decompositions are both
   728→   728	instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
   729→   729	image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
   730→   730	"
   731→   731	
   732→   732	DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
   733→   733	DisabledTheorem funext Fin.cases Fin.eq_zero_or_eq_succ

   735→   735	
   736→   736	World "PsetFin"
   737→   737	Level 13
   738→   738	
   739→   739	Title "The succAbove Unification"
   740→   740	
   741→   741	Introduction "
   742→   742	# Two Decompositions, One Operation
   743→   743	
   744→   744	In Levels 11-12, you proved two decompositions of `Fin (n + 1)`:
   745→   745	- **castSucc/last**: every element is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`
   746→   746	- **0/succ**: every element is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j`
   747→   747	
   748→   748	Level 12's conclusion mentioned `Fin.succAbove p` — an embedding
   749→   749	`Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that 'skips' position `p`. Now you'll prove
   750→   750	the two key facts that unify the decompositions:
   751→   751	
   752→   752	1. `Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc` — skipping the last position IS castSucc
   753→   753	2. `Fin.succAbove 0 = Fin.succ` — skipping position 0 IS succ
   754→   754	
   755→   755	This turns a pattern observation into a theorem: both `castSucc`
   756→   756	and `succ` are special cases of the single 'skip one position'
   757→   757	operation `succAbove`.
   758→   758	
   759→   759	**Your task**: Prove both equalities.
   760→   760	"
   761→   761	
   762→   762	/-- succAbove at last is castSucc, and succAbove at 0 is succ. -/
   763→   763	Statement (n : ℕ) :
   764→   764	    Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc ∧
   765→   765	    Fin.succAbove (0 : Fin (n + 1)) = Fin.succ := by
   766→   766	  Hint "Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
   767→   767	  constructor
   768→   768	  · Hint "The first part is `Fin.succAbove_last` — skipping the last
   769→   769	    position gives `castSucc`.
   770→   770	    Use `exact Fin.succAbove_last`."
   771→   771	    exact Fin.succAbove_last
   772→   772	  · Hint "The second part is `Fin.succAbove_zero` — skipping position 0
   773→   773	    gives `succ`.
   774→   774	    Use `exact Fin.succAbove_zero`."
   775→   775	    exact Fin.succAbove_zero
   776→   776	
   777→   777	Conclusion "
   778→   778	You've proved the **succAbove unification**: both `castSucc` and `succ`
   779→   779	are special cases of `Fin.succAbove p`.
   780→   780	
   781→   781	| Boundary | `p` | `succAbove p` | Decomposition |
   782→   782	|---|---|---|---|
   783→   783	| Top | `Fin.last n` | `Fin.castSucc` | castSucc/last |
   784→   784	| Bottom | `0` | `Fin.succ` | 0/succ |
   785→   785	
   786→   786	This is the mathematical punchline of Phase 1's embedding story.
   787→   787	The two decompositions you proved in L11-L12 aren't just 'dual' — they're
   788→   788	instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
   789→   789	image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
   790→   790	
   791→   791	The general decomposition `Fin (n+1) = succAbove p '' Fin n ∪ {p}`
   792→   792	holds for ANY `p : Fin (n+1)`, not just the endpoints. This means
   793→   793	you can decompose `Fin (n+1)` by skipping any position, not just
   794→   794	the first or last. The endpoint cases are the ones with the cleanest
   795→   795	names (`castSucc`, `succ`), which is why the course started there.
   796→   796	"
   797→   797	
   798→   798	/-- `Fin.succAbove_last` states that
   799→   799	`Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc`.
   800→   800	
   801→   801	Skipping the last position is the same as `castSucc` — the embedding
   802→   802	that includes `Fin n` into `Fin (n+1)` by leaving the value unchanged.
   803→   803	-/
   804→   804	TheoremDoc Fin.succAbove_last as "Fin.succAbove_last" in "Fin"
   805→   805	
   806→   806	/-- `Fin.succAbove_zero` states that
   807→   807	`Fin.succAbove (0 : Fin (n+1)) = Fin.succ`.
   808→   808	
   809→   809	Skipping position 0 is the same as `succ` — the embedding that shifts
   810→   810	every value up by 1.
   811→   811	-/
   812→   812	TheoremDoc Fin.succAbove_zero as "Fin.succAbove_zero" in "Fin"
   813→   813	
   814→   814	/-- `Fin.succAbove p` is the order-preserving embedding
   815→   815	`Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that skips position `p`.
   816→   816	
   817→   817	It unifies `castSucc` and `succ`:
   818→   818	- `succAbove (last n) = castSucc`
   819→   819	- `succAbove 0 = succ`
   820→   820	-/
   821→   821	DefinitionDoc Fin.succAbove as "Fin.succAbove"
   822→   822	
   823→   823	NewTheorem Fin.succAbove_last Fin.succAbove_zero
   824→   824	NewDefinition Fin.succAbove

   829→   829	
   830→   830	World "PsetFin"
   831→   831	Level 14
   832→   832	
   833→   833	Title "Cyclic Permutation Has No Fixed Points"
   834→   834	
   835→   835	Introduction "
   836→   836	# A Cyclic Permutation
   837→   837	
   838→   838	In MeetFin, you saw that `Fin n` supports modular arithmetic —
   839→   839	addition wraps around. A natural example of this wrapping is the
   840→   840	**cyclic permutation** `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.
   841→   841	
   842→   842	This function shifts every element forward by one position, with
   843→   843	the last element wrapping back to 0:
   844→   844	- `0 -> 1`
   845→   845	- `1 -> 2`
   846→   846	- `2 -> 0`
   847→   847	
   848→   848	A **fixed point** of a function `f` is an element `i` such that
   849→   849	`f i = i` — it doesn't move. The cyclic permutation has **no**
   850→   850	fixed points: every element moves.
   851→   851	
   852→   852	**Your task**: Prove that `![1, 2, 0]` has no fixed points.
   853→   853	
   854→   854	The strategy is case analysis on `Fin 3`: check each of the three
   855→   855	elements and verify that the output differs from the input.
   856→   856	"
   857→   857	
   858→   858	/-- The cyclic permutation ![1, 2, 0] has no fixed points. -/
   859→   859	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) i ≠ i := by
   860→   860	  Hint "Start with `intro i` to take an arbitrary element of `Fin 3`,
   861→   861	  then destructure with `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   862→   862	  intro ⟨v, hv⟩
   863→   863	  Hint "Case-split on `v` to check each value:
   864→   864	  `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
   865→   865	  Hint (hidden := true) "For each valid value, the output differs
   866→   866	  from the input. Close with `intro h; cases h` for the contradiction."
   867→   867	  cases v with
   868→   868	  | zero =>
   869→   869	    Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1 /= 0`.
   870→   870	    Use `intro h; cases h`."
   871→   871	    intro h; cases h
   872→   872	  | succ n =>
   873→   873	    cases n with
   874→   874	    | zero =>
   875→   875	      Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2 /= 1`.
   876→   876	      Use `intro h; cases h`."
   877→   877	      intro h; cases h
   878→   878	    | succ m =>
   879→   879	      cases m with
   880→   880	      | zero =>
   881→   881	        Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0 /= 2`.
   882→   882	        Use `intro h; cases h`."
   883→   883	        intro h; cases h
   884→   884	      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   885→   885	
   886→   886	Conclusion "
   887→   887	You proved that the cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` has no fixed
   888→   888	points — every element moves.
   889→   889	
   890→   890	The proof used the same case-analysis-on-Fin pattern you've used
   891→   891	throughout Phase 1: destructure into `(v, hv)`, case-split on `v`,
   892→   892	and handle each valid case.
   893→   893	
   894→   894	This is the cyclic structure mentioned in MeetFin's modular
   895→   895	arithmetic level: the permutation `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` cycles
   896→   896	through all three positions before returning to the start.
   897→   897	Algebraically, this permutation generates the cyclic group
   898→   898	$\\mathbb{Z}/3\\mathbb{Z}$ — the same group structure as
   899→   899	addition modulo 3 on `Fin 3`.
   900→   900	
   901→   901	This level exercises three skills on a single object:
   902→   902	- `vec_cons` evaluation (FinTuples) — reading `![1, 2, 0]` at each index
   903→   903	- Case analysis on `Fin 3` (MeetFin) — enumerating all elements
   904→   904	- Inequality via `cases h` (MeetFin) — proving the output differs
   905→   905	"

   913→   913	
   914→   914	Title "Obtain Meets Decomposition"
   915→   915	
   916→   916	Introduction "
   917→   917	# Combining obtain with Decomposition
   918→   918	
   919→   919	In Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
   920→   920	In Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
   921→   921	existential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).
   922→   922	
   923→   923	Here you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
   924→   924	image, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
   925→   925	element can't be `Fin.last`.
   926→   926	
   927→   927	This is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
   928→   928	there you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
   929→   929	you prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.
   930→   930	
   931→   931	**Strategy**:
   932→   932	1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
   933→   933	2. Rewrite using the equation
   934→   934	3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
   935→   935	"
   936→   936	
   937→   937	/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
   938→   938	Statement (n : ℕ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
   939→   939	    i ≠ Fin.last n := by
   940→   940	  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h`.
   941→   941	  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
   942→   942	  obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h
   943→   943	  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
   944→   944	  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
   945→   945	  rw [hj]
   946→   946	  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
   947→   947	  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
   948→   948	  via value reasoning."
   949→   949	  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
   950→   950	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
   951→   951	  intro heq
   952→   952	  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
   953→   953	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
   954→   954	  omega
   955→   955	
   956→   956	Conclusion "
   957→   957	You combined two skills:
   958→   958	1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
   959→   959	2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
   960→   960	   the contradiction
   961→   961	
   962→   962	This is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
   963→   963	gives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
   964→   964	need to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
   965→   965	equation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here — instead
   966→   966	of constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.
   967→   967	
   968→   968	The fact you just proved — castSucc images are never last — is
   969→   969	the converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
   970→   970	non-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
   971→   971	an element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
   972→   972	"

   981→   981	Title "Simple Pigeonhole: Two Pigeons, One Hole"
   982→   982	
   983→   983	Introduction "
   984→   984	# The Simplest Pigeonhole
   985→   985	
   986→   986	The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
   987→   987	fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.
   988→   988	
   989→   989	Before tackling the full 3-pigeons-2-holes version, let's practice
   990→   990	the pattern on the simplest case: **2 pigeons, 1 hole**.
   991→   991	
   992→   992	Any function `f : Fin 2 -> Fin 1` must send two different inputs
   993→   993	to the same output. Why? Because `Fin 1` has only ONE element
   994→   994	(the value `0`), so both `f 0` and `f 1` must equal `0`.
   995→   995	
   996→   996	**Strategy**:
   997→   997	1. The witnesses are `0` and `1` (the two inputs)
   998→   998	2. They're different (the inequality `0 /= 1`)
   999→   999	3. Both map to the unique output

   508	World "PsetFin"
   509	Level 10
   510	
   511	Title "Modular Equality"
   512	
   513	Introduction "
   514	# When Different Formulas Agree
   515	
   516	Two functions on `Fin 5` are described by different-looking formulas:
   517	- `f i = i + 3`
   518	- `g i = i + 8`
   519	
   520	Are they the same function? In ordinary arithmetic, adding 3 and
   521	adding 8 give different results. But in `Fin 5`, the arithmetic wraps
   522	around: `8 mod 5 = 3`, so `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. This means
   523	`i + 8 = i + 3` for every `i : Fin 5`.
   524	
   525	**Your task**: Prove `f = g` using `ext` and the formula rewrites.
   526	"
   527	
   528	/-- Two functions with the same modular formula are equal. -/
   529	Statement (f g : Fin 5 → Fin 5)
   530	    (hf : ∀ i : Fin 5, f i = i + 3)
   531	    (hg : ∀ i : Fin 5, g i = i + 8) : f = g := by
   532	  Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
   533	  Use `ext i` to introduce the index."
   534	  ext i
   535	  Hint "Substitute both formulas: `rw [hf, hg]`.
   536	  The resulting equality holds because `(3 : Fin 5) = (8 : Fin 5)`
   537	  by modular arithmetic."
   538	  Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal becomes
   539	  `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. Since `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`
   540	  by modular arithmetic, `rfl` closes it."
   541	  rw [hf, hg]
   542	  Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides are definitionally equal because
   543	  `(8 : Fin 5) = (3 : Fin 5)`. Use `rfl`."
   544	  rfl
   545	
   546	Conclusion "
   547	After rewriting, the goal was `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`. This is
   548	true by `rfl` because `(8 : Fin 5)` and `(3 : Fin 5)` are the
   549	**same element** — `8 mod 5 = 3`.
   550	
   551	This combines three skills:
   552	- **Function extensionality** (`ext`) from FinTuples
   553	- **Universally quantified rewrite** (`rw [hf, hg]`) from FinTuples
   554	- **Modular arithmetic** from MeetFin Level 19
   555	
   556	**Why does `rfl` close `i + 3 = i + 8` in `Fin 5`?** The numerals
   557	`3` and `8` in `Fin 5` are *the same element*: both reduce to the
   558	element with value `3` (since `8 mod 5 = 3`). Lean computes this
   559	reduction during elaboration, so the two sides are definitionally
   560	equal — no proof step required. This is the power of modular
   561	arithmetic being built into the type: Lean *knows* that `(8 : Fin 5)`
   562	and `(3 : Fin 5)` are identical.
   563	
   564	In a pset, recognizing which skills to combine is the real challenge.
   565	"

   571	World "PsetFin"
   572	Level 11
   573	
   574	Title "The General Decomposition"
   575	
   576	Introduction "
   577	# From Concrete to Abstract
   578	
   579	In the FinNavigation boss, you proved that every element of
   580	`Fin 3` is either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   581	That proof used exhaustive case analysis on all three values.
   582	
   583	Here you'll prove the **general** version: for ANY `n`, every
   584	element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc`.
   585	
   586	The proof uses a different strategy. Instead of checking each
   587	value, you split on whether `i.val = n` or `i.val < n`:
   588	- If `i.val = n`: then `i = Fin.last n` (by `ext` + `Fin.val_last`)
   589	- If `i.val < n`: then `i = j.castSucc` where `j = (i.val, proof)` (the L13 pattern from FinNavigation)
   590	
   591	The key insight: `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) means either
   592	`i.val < n` or `i.val = n`. You can derive this disjunction
   593	with `omega` and then case-split on it.
   594	"
   595	
   596	/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either last or a castSucc image. -/
   597	Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = Fin.last n ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
   598	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   599	  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   600	  intro i
   601	  cases i with
   602	  | mk v hv =>
   603	    Hint "Now `v < n + 1`. This means either `v < n` or `v = n`.
   604	    Create this disjunction: `have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`."
   605	    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h : v < n or v = n := by omega`"
   606	    have h : v < n ∨ v = n := by omega
   607	    Hint "Case-split on `h`: `cases h with | inl hlt => ... | inr heq => ...`"
   608	    cases h with
   609	    | inl hlt =>
   610	      Hint "Here `v < n`, so the element is a `castSucc` image.
   611	      Choose `right` and provide the witness."
   612	      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨v, hlt⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
   613	      right
   614	      use ⟨v, hlt⟩
   615	      ext
   616	      rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   617	    | inr heq =>
   618	      Hint "Here `v = n`, so the element IS `Fin.last n`.
   619	      Choose `left` and prove the equality."
   620	      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
   621	      left
   622	      ext
   623	      rw [Fin.val_last]
   624	      exact heq
   625	
   626	Conclusion "
   627	You've generalized the concrete decomposition to ALL `n`.
   628	
   629	The concrete proof (FinNavigation boss) worked by checking every
   630	value of `Fin 3`. The abstract proof works by splitting on
   631	`v < n or v = n` — a fact `omega` can derive from `v < n + 1`.
   632	
   633	Notice the **value repackaging** pattern (from FinNavigation L13):
   634	`use ⟨v, hlt⟩` takes the same value `v` and justifies it in
   635	`Fin n` with the new bound `hlt : v < n`. The value doesn't
   636	change — only the type's upper bound shrinks.
   637	
   638	This is the first taste of **generalizing from concrete to
   639	abstract**: the same structural fact holds, but the proof strategy
   640	changes from exhaustive enumeration to case analysis on a
   641	disjunction. This pattern appears throughout mathematics — concrete
   642	examples reveal the structure, but general proofs use different
   643	(often cleaner) strategies.
   644	
   645	(In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.lastCases` —
   646	an eliminator that lets you handle the last element and castSucc
   647	images separately. You just proved the underlying fact by hand.)
   648	"

   654	World "PsetFin"
   655	Level 12
   656	
   657	Title "The Dual Decomposition"
   658	
   659	Introduction "
   660	# General 0/succ Decomposition
   661	
   662	In Level 11, you proved the general **castSucc/last decomposition**:
   663	every element of `Fin (n + 1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
   664	`j.castSucc` for some `j`.
   665	
   666	The course has emphasized that castSucc/last and 0/succ are **dual**
   667	decompositions — mirror images of each other. But so far, you've
   668	only proved the 0/succ decomposition concretely for `Fin 3`
   669	(FinNavigation Level 10). Here you'll prove it for general `n`.
   670	
   671	The proof mirrors L11's structure: instead of splitting on
   672	`v = n` vs `v < n`, you split on `v = 0` vs `v >= 1`.
   673	- If `v = 0`: the element is `0`
   674	- If `v >= 1`: the element is `j.succ` where `j = (v - 1, proof)`
   675	
   676	**Your task**: Prove the general 0/succ decomposition.
   677	"
   678	
   679	/-- Every element of Fin (n+1) is either 0 or a successor. -/
   680	Statement (n : ℕ) : ∀ i : Fin (n + 1), i = 0 ∨ ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.succ := by
   681	  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure with
   682	  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   683	  intro i
   684	  cases i with
   685	  | mk v hv =>
   686	    Hint "Now case-split on `v` itself: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
   687	    cases v with
   688	    | zero =>
   689	      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
   690	      Hint (hidden := true) "`left; rfl`"
   691	      left
   692	      rfl
   693	    | succ m =>
   694	      Hint "Here `v = m + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where `j = ⟨m, ...⟩ : Fin n`.
   695	      Choose `right`, provide the witness, then prove via `ext` + `Fin.val_succ`."
   696	      Hint (hidden := true) "`right; use ⟨m, by omega⟩; ext; rw [Fin.val_succ]`"
   697	      right
   698	      use ⟨m, by omega⟩
   699	      ext
   700	      rw [Fin.val_succ]
   701	
   702	Conclusion "
   703	You've completed the **dual** of Level 11:
   704	
   705	| Decomposition | General proof | Splits on |
   706	|---|---|---|
   707	| castSucc/last (L11) | `v = n` or `v < n` | Top boundary |
   708	| 0/succ (this level) | `v = 0` or `v >= 1` | Bottom boundary |
   709	
   710	The **value repackaging** pattern appeared again: `use ⟨m, by omega⟩`
   711	takes the predecessor value and repackages it into `Fin n`.
   712	
   713	Both decompositions are now proved for arbitrary `n`. This
   714	symmetry is fundamental: it's why tuples can be built from
   715	*either end* — `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ` (prepend),
   716	`vecSnoc` routes through `castSucc`/`last` (append).
   717	
   718	In Mathlib, this decomposition is packaged as `Fin.cases` —
   719	the 0/succ dual of `Fin.lastCases`.
   720	
   721	There's a unifying concept behind both decompositions:
   722	`Fin.succAbove p` is an embedding `Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that
   723	'skips' position `p`. When `p = Fin.last n`, `succAbove` is
   724	`castSucc` (skip the last position). When `p = 0`, `succAbove`
   725	is `succ` (skip position 0). Both `castSucc` and `succ` are
   726	special cases of the same 'skip one position' operation. This
   727	means the castSucc/last and 0/succ decompositions are both
   728	instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
   729	image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
   730	"
   731

   736	World "PsetFin"
   737	Level 13
   738	
   739	Title "The succAbove Unification"
   740	
   741	Introduction "
   742	# Two Decompositions, One Operation
   743	
   744	In Levels 11-12, you proved two decompositions of `Fin (n + 1)`:
   745	- **castSucc/last**: every element is either `Fin.last n` or `j.castSucc` for some `j`
   746	- **0/succ**: every element is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j`
   747	
   748	Level 12's conclusion mentioned `Fin.succAbove p` — an embedding
   749	`Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that 'skips' position `p`. Now you'll prove
   750	the two key facts that unify the decompositions:
   751	
   752	1. `Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc` — skipping the last position IS castSucc
   753	2. `Fin.succAbove 0 = Fin.succ` — skipping position 0 IS succ
   754	
   755	This turns a pattern observation into a theorem: both `castSucc`
   756	and `succ` are special cases of the single 'skip one position'
   757	operation `succAbove`.
   758	
   759	**Your task**: Prove both equalities.
   760	"
   761	
   762	/-- succAbove at last is castSucc, and succAbove at 0 is succ. -/
   763	Statement (n : ℕ) :
   764	    Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc ∧
   765	    Fin.succAbove (0 : Fin (n + 1)) = Fin.succ := by
   766	  Hint "Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
   767	  constructor
   768	  · Hint "The first part is `Fin.succAbove_last` — skipping the last
   769	    position gives `castSucc`.
   770	    Use `exact Fin.succAbove_last`."
   771	    exact Fin.succAbove_last
   772	  · Hint "The second part is `Fin.succAbove_zero` — skipping position 0
   773	    gives `succ`.
   774	    Use `exact Fin.succAbove_zero`."
   775	    exact Fin.succAbove_zero
   776	
   777	Conclusion "
   778	You've proved the **succAbove unification**: both `castSucc` and `succ`
   779	are special cases of `Fin.succAbove p`.
   780	
   781	| Boundary | `p` | `succAbove p` | Decomposition |
   782	|---|---|---|---|
   783	| Top | `Fin.last n` | `Fin.castSucc` | castSucc/last |
   784	| Bottom | `0` | `Fin.succ` | 0/succ |
   785	
   786	This is the mathematical punchline of Phase 1's embedding story.
   787	The two decompositions you proved in L11-L12 aren't just 'dual' — they're
   788	instances of a single pattern: decompose `Fin (n+1)` into the
   789	image of `succAbove p` plus the singleton `{p}`.
   790	
   791	The general decomposition `Fin (n+1) = succAbove p '' Fin n ∪ {p}`
   792	holds for ANY `p : Fin (n+1)`, not just the endpoints. This means
   793	you can decompose `Fin (n+1)` by skipping any position, not just
   794	the first or last. The endpoint cases are the ones with the cleanest
   795	names (`castSucc`, `succ`), which is why the course started there.
   796	"
   797	
   798	/-- `Fin.succAbove_last` states that
   799	`Fin.succAbove (Fin.last n) = Fin.castSucc`.
   800	
   801	Skipping the last position is the same as `castSucc` — the embedding
   802	that includes `Fin n` into `Fin (n+1)` by leaving the value unchanged.
   803	-/
   804	TheoremDoc Fin.succAbove_last as "Fin.succAbove_last" in "Fin"
   805	
   806	/-- `Fin.succAbove_zero` states that
   807	`Fin.succAbove (0 : Fin (n+1)) = Fin.succ`.
   808	
   809	Skipping position 0 is the same as `succ` — the embedding that shifts
   810	every value up by 1.
   811	-/
   812	TheoremDoc Fin.succAbove_zero as "Fin.succAbove_zero" in "Fin"
   813	
   814	/-- `Fin.succAbove p` is the order-preserving embedding
   815	`Fin n -> Fin (n+1)` that skips position `p`.
   816	
   817	It unifies `castSucc` and `succ`:
   818	- `succAbove (last n) = castSucc`
   819	- `succAbove 0 = succ`
   820	-/
   821	DefinitionDoc Fin.succAbove as "Fin.succAbove"
   822	
   823	NewTheorem Fin.succAbove_last Fin.succAbove_zero
   824	NewDefinition Fin.succAbove

   830	World "PsetFin"
   831	Level 14
   832	
   833	Title "Cyclic Permutation Has No Fixed Points"
   834	
   835	Introduction "
   836	# A Cyclic Permutation
   837	
   838	In MeetFin, you saw that `Fin n` supports modular arithmetic —
   839	addition wraps around. A natural example of this wrapping is the
   840	**cyclic permutation** `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.
   841	
   842	This function shifts every element forward by one position, with
   843	the last element wrapping back to 0:
   844	- `0 -> 1`
   845	- `1 -> 2`
   846	- `2 -> 0`
   847	
   848	A **fixed point** of a function `f` is an element `i` such that
   849	`f i = i` — it doesn't move. The cyclic permutation has **no**
   850	fixed points: every element moves.
   851	
   852	**Your task**: Prove that `![1, 2, 0]` has no fixed points.
   853	
   854	The strategy is case analysis on `Fin 3`: check each of the three
   855	elements and verify that the output differs from the input.
   856	"
   857	
   858	/-- The cyclic permutation ![1, 2, 0] has no fixed points. -/
   859	Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 → Fin 3) i ≠ i := by
   860	  Hint "Start with `intro i` to take an arbitrary element of `Fin 3`,
   861	  then destructure with `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
   862	  intro ⟨v, hv⟩
   863	  Hint "Case-split on `v` to check each value:
   864	  `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
   865	  Hint (hidden := true) "For each valid value, the output differs
   866	  from the input. Close with `intro h; cases h` for the contradiction."
   867	  cases v with
   868	  | zero =>
   869	    Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1 /= 0`.
   870	    Use `intro h; cases h`."
   871	    intro h; cases h
   872	  | succ n =>
   873	    cases n with
   874	    | zero =>
   875	      Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2 /= 1`.
   876	      Use `intro h; cases h`."
   877	      intro h; cases h
   878	    | succ m =>
   879	      cases m with
   880	      | zero =>
   881	        Hint (hidden := true) "`![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0 /= 2`.
   882	        Use `intro h; cases h`."
   883	        intro h; cases h
   884	      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
   885	
   886	Conclusion "
   887	You proved that the cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` has no fixed
   888	points — every element moves.
   889	
   890	The proof used the same case-analysis-on-Fin pattern you've used
   891	throughout Phase 1: destructure into `(v, hv)`, case-split on `v`,
   892	and handle each valid case.
   893	
   894	This is the cyclic structure mentioned in MeetFin's modular
   895	arithmetic level: the permutation `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` cycles
   896	through all three positions before returning to the start.
   897	Algebraically, this permutation generates the cyclic group
   898	$\\mathbb{Z}/3\\mathbb{Z}$ — the same group structure as
   899	addition modulo 3 on `Fin 3`.
   900	
   901	This level exercises three skills on a single object:
   902	- `vec_cons` evaluation (FinTuples) — reading `![1, 2, 0]` at each index
   903	- Case analysis on `Fin 3` (MeetFin) — enumerating all elements
   904	- Inequality via `cases h` (MeetFin) — proving the output differs
   905	"

   914	Title "Obtain Meets Decomposition"
   915	
   916	Introduction "
   917	# Combining obtain with Decomposition
   918	
   919	In Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
   920	In Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
   921	existential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).
   922	
   923	Here you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
   924	image, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
   925	element can't be `Fin.last`.
   926	
   927	This is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
   928	there you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
   929	you prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.
   930	
   931	**Strategy**:
   932	1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
   933	2. Rewrite using the equation
   934	3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
   935	"
   936	
   937	/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
   938	Statement (n : ℕ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : ∃ j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
   939	    i ≠ Fin.last n := by
   940	  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h`.
   941	  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
   942	  obtain ⟨j, hj⟩ := h
   943	  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
   944	  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
   945	  rw [hj]
   946	  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
   947	  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
   948	  via value reasoning."
   949	  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
   950	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
   951	  intro heq
   952	  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
   953	  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
   954	  omega
   955	
   956	Conclusion "
   957	You combined two skills:
   958	1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
   959	2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
   960	   the contradiction
   961	
   962	This is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
   963	gives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
   964	need to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
   965	equation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here — instead
   966	of constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.
   967	
   968	The fact you just proved — castSucc images are never last — is
   969	the converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
   970	non-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
   971	an element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
   972	"

   981	Title "Simple Pigeonhole: Two Pigeons, One Hole"
   982	
   983	Introduction "
   984	# The Simplest Pigeonhole
   985	
   986	The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
   987	fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.
   988	
   989	Before tackling the full 3-pigeons-2-holes version, let's practice
   990	the pattern on the simplest case: **2 pigeons, 1 hole**.
   991	
   992	Any function `f : Fin 2 -> Fin 1` must send two different inputs
   993	to the same output. Why? Because `Fin 1` has only ONE element
   994	(the value `0`), so both `f 0` and `f 1` must equal `0`.
   995	
   996	**Strategy**:
   997	1. The witnesses are `0` and `1` (the two inputs)
   998	2. They're different (the inequality `0 /= 1`)
   999	3. Both map to the unique output

  1003→  1003	- So `f 0 = 0` and `f 1 = 0` (compare)
  1004→  1004	- Therefore `f 0 = f 1` (collision found)
  1005→  1005	"
  1006→  1006

  1010→  1010	  First classify `f 0` and `f 1`:

  1013→  1013	  `f 0 = 0` (the only possible value). Similarly for `f 1`.
  1014→  1014	  Then `use 0, 1`."
  1015→  1015	  have h0 : f 0 = 0 := by ext; omega
  1016→  1016	  have h1 : f 1 = 0 := by ext; omega
  1017→  1017	  Hint "Now provide the witnesses. `use 0, 1` says: the collision

  1019→  1019	  use 0, 1

  1030→  1030	The simplest pigeonhole: with only ONE hole, every pigeon goes in

  1033→  1033	The proof pattern was:

  1053→  1053	
  1054→  1054	Introduction "
  1055→  1055	# Three Pigeons, Two Holes
  1056→  1056	
  1057→  1057	The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
  1058→  1058	fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.
  1059→  1059	
  1060→  1060	Formally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
  1061→  1061	different inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
  1062→  1062	only 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.
  1063→  1063	
  1064→  1064	This is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** — and
  1065→  1065	you can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.
  1066→  1066	
  1067→  1067	**Strategy**:
  1068→  1068	1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
  1069→  1069	2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
  1070→  1070	3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
  1071→  1071	4. If they differ: check `f 2` — it must match one of them
  1072→  1072	"
  1073→  1073	
  1074→  1074	/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
  1075→  1075	Statement (f : Fin 3 → Fin 2) : ∃ i j : Fin 3, i ≠ j ∧ f i = f j := by
  1076→  1076	  Hint "**Full strategy overview** — the proof has three phases:
  1077→  1077	
  1078→  1078	  1. **Classify**: Prove a helper `classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1`
  1079→  1079	  2. **Apply**: Use `classify` on `f 0`, `f 1`, and (when needed) `f 2`
  1080→  1080	  3. **Case tree**: 6 branches, each ending with `use i, j; constructor; intro h; cases h; rw [...]`
  1081→  1081	
  1082→  1082	  If `f 0` and `f 1` have the same value: collision at `0, 1`.
  1083→  1083	  If they differ: check `f 2` — it must match one of them."
  1084→  1084	  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
  1085→  1085	  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.

  1090→  1090	  have classify : ∀ x : Fin 2, x = 0 ∨ x = 1 := by
  1091→  1091	    intro ⟨v, hv⟩
  1092→  1092	    cases v with
  1093→  1093	    | zero => left; rfl
  1094→  1094	    | succ n =>
  1095→  1095	      cases n with
  1096→  1096	      | zero => right; rfl

  1098→  1098	  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:

  1103→  1103	
  1104→  1104	  - `f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0`: collision at 0 and 1 (done)
  1105→  1105	  - `f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1`: check `f 2` — matches 0 or 1
  1106→  1106	  - `f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1`: collision at 0 and 1 (done)

  1183→  1183	Conclusion "
  1184→  1184	You've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
  1185→  1185	any function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
  1186→  1186	a collision.
  1187→  1187	
  1188→  1188	The proof strategy was:
  1189→  1189	1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
  1190→  1190	2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
  1191→  1191	3. **Find** two inputs with the same output
  1192→  1192	
  1193→  1193	Every tool in this proof came from Phase 1:
  1194→  1194	- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
  1195→  1195	- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
  1196→  1196	- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
  1197→  1197	- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)
  1198→  1198	
  1199→  1199	**The abstract pigeonhole principle**: This generalizes. For any
  1200→  1200	function `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`, there must exist `i j` with
  1201→  1201	`i /= j` and `f i = f j`. More inputs than outputs forces a
  1202→  1202	collision — always. The concrete proof here used exhaustive case
  1203→  1203	analysis, which doesn't scale: for `Fin 100 -> Fin 99`, you'd
  1204→  1204	need astronomical case splits. In the Finset worlds ahead, you'll
  1205→  1205	learn cardinality tools that prove pigeonhole for any `n` in a
  1206→  1206	few lines.
  1207→  1207	
  1208→  1208	**The cardinality consequence**: If there were a collision-free
  1209→  1209	function from `Fin 3` to `Fin 2`, we could embed a 3-element set
  1210→  1210	into a 2-element set — which is impossible. So `|Fin 3| > |Fin 2|`,
  1211→  1211	confirming that `Fin 3` is genuinely 'larger than' `Fin 2`. More
  1212→  1212	importantly, the parameter `n` in `Fin n` is *recoverable* from
  1213→  1213	the type: `Fin n` and `Fin m` can only be equivalent (have a
  1214→  1214	bijection between them) when `n = m`. This is what makes `Fin n`
  1215→  1215	the canonical finite type — it uniquely represents 'a type with
  1216→  1216	exactly n elements.'
  1217→  1217	"
