   892	DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right Finset.mem_inter_of_mem Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.inter_subset_left Finset.inter_subset_right Finset.union_comm Finset.inter_comm sup_comm inf_comm
   893	import Game.Metadata
   894	
   895	World "FinsetOperations"
   896	Level 9
   897	
   898	Title "Intersection Idempotency"
   899	
   900	Introduction "
   901	# Your First ext Identity: s ∩ s = s
   902	
   903	The simplest set identity you can prove with `ext`: intersecting a
   904	set with itself gives back the same set.
   905	
   906	After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_inter]`, the goal becomes:
   907	$$x \\in s \\land x \\in s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
   908	
   909	**New notation**: `.1` and `.2` on conjunction hypotheses.
   910	
   911	When you have `h : P ∧ Q`, you can extract:
   912	- `h.1` gives the first component (`P`)
   913	- `h.2` gives the second component (`Q`)
   914	
   915	And to *build* a conjunction, use anonymous constructors:
   916	`⟨proof_of_P, proof_of_Q⟩` is shorthand for `And.intro proof_of_P proof_of_Q`.
   917	
   918	**Forward**: given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, project with `h.1`.
   919	
   920	**Backward**: given `hs : x ∈ s`, duplicate with `⟨hs, hs⟩`.
   921	
   922	**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cap s = s$.
   923	"
   924	
   925	/-- Intersection is idempotent: s ∩ s = s. -/
   926	Statement (s : Finset ℕ) : s ∩ s = s := by
   927	  Hint "Start with `ext x` to convert the equality into a
   928	  membership biconditional."
   929	  ext x
   930	  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to unfold the intersection."
   931	  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
   932	  Hint "The goal is `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s ↔ x ∈ s`.
   933	  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
   934	  constructor
   935	  · Hint "Given `h : x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s`.
   936	    Use `intro h` and then `exact h.1` to project the first component."
   937	    intro h
   938	    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h.1` — the `.1` projection extracts
   939	    the first component of the conjunction."
   940	    exact h.1
   941	  · Hint "Given `hs : x ∈ s`, you need `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s`.
   942	    Use `intro hs` and then `exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` to build the conjunction."
   943	    intro hs
   944	    Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨hs, hs⟩` — the anonymous constructor
   945	    builds the conjunction by duplicating the same proof."
   946	    exact ⟨hs, hs⟩
   947	
   948	Conclusion "
   949	$s \\cap s = s$ — intersection is **idempotent**.
   950	
   951	The proof introduced two key notations:
   952	- **`.1` and `.2`**: extract components from a conjunction hypothesis.
   953	  If `h : P ∧ Q`, then `h.1 : P` and `h.2 : Q`.
   954	- **`⟨a, b⟩`**: build a conjunction in one line. `⟨hs, hs⟩` builds
   955	  `x ∈ s ∧ x ∈ s` by using the same proof twice.
   956	
   957	You already know `constructor` splits a conjunction goal into two
   958	subgoals. The anonymous constructor `⟨a, b⟩` is a one-line alternative
   959	when both components are short.
   960	
   961	This was the simplest `ext` identity: the logic reduced to
   962	$P \\land P \\iff P$. Next you'll see identities where the logic is
   963	more interesting.
   964	"
   965	
   966	DisabledTactic trivial «decide» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
   967	DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right Finset.mem_inter_of_mem Finset.mem_of_mem_inter_left Finset.mem_of_mem_inter_right Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.inter_subset_left Finset.inter_subset_right Finset.union_comm Finset.inter_comm sup_comm inf_comm inf_le_left inf_le_right le_sup_left le_sup_right Finset.inter_self Finset.union_self inf_idem sup_idem Finset.union_empty Finset.empty_union sup_bot_eq bot_sup_eq and_self
   968	import Game.Metadata
   969	
   970	World "FinsetOperations"
   971	Level 10
   972	
   973	Title "Empty Set Identity"
   974	
   975	Introduction "
   976	# Union with the Empty Set
   977	
   978	Another set identity using `ext`: prove that
   979	$s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.
   980	
   981	Unioning with the empty set adds nothing — every element of `s ∪ ∅`
   982	is either in `s` (which contributes) or in `∅` (which contributes
   983	nothing, since `∅` has no elements).
   984	
   985	After `ext x` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, the goal becomes:
   986	$$x \\in s \\lor x \\in \\varnothing \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; x \\in s$$
   987	
   988	**Forward**: If `x ∈ s ∨ x ∈ ∅`, case-split. In the `x ∈ s` case,
   989	done. In the `x ∈ ∅` case, this is impossible — recall from Finset
   990	Basics that `Finset.notMem_empty x` proves `x ∉ ∅`.
   991	
   992	**Backward**: If `x ∈ s`, choose `left`.
   993	
   994	**Recall**: `exact absurd he (Finset.notMem_empty x)` closes a goal
   995	when you have `he : x ∈ ∅`. The function `absurd` (from MeetFin) derives
   996	anything from a proof and its negation: since `he` says `x ∈ ∅` and
   997	`Finset.notMem_empty x` says `x ∉ ∅`, the contradiction closes the goal.
   998	
   999	**Your task**: Prove that $s \\cup \\varnothing = s$.

content.thinking:
I now have a thorough understanding of all 10 worlds. Let me also read the remaining parts of FinsetOperations, FinsetImage, Cardinality, and AbstractionLadder to fully understand their content, specifically the later levels.

Actually, I have enough to write a comprehensive enrichment review. Let me summarize what I've seen across all 10 worlds:

**MeetFin** (23 levels): Fin n introduction, constructors, val, ext, equality, proof irrelevance, general Fin n, empty type, explosion, unique element, destructuring, two elements, case analysis, modular arithmetic, concrete application, existential witness, boss.


**FinNavigation** (16 levels): Fin.last, Fin.castSucc, Fin.succ, val lemmas, separation facts, injectivity, 0/succ decomposition, succ never zero, recognizing last, finding castSucc preimage, surjectivity, boss.
**FinTuples** (21 levels): Functions as tuples, vec notation, building tuples, Fin.cons, access lemmas, Fin.tail, head-tail reconstruction, round-trip, vecSnoc, init, init-snoc reconstruction, ext for functions, tuple composition, tuples as data, cons injectivity, snoc injectivity, empty.

Now moving into tuple equality proofs, flipping equations, and reconstruction strategies before the boss level. **PsetFin** (18 levels) covers a problem set applying Fin skills across double witnesses, castSucc properties, snoc reconstruction, antisymmetric equality, pointwise formulas, vacuous truth, succ never zero, obtain tactics, zero-succ retrieval, modular equality, general decomposition, succAbove unification, cyclic permutations, obtain with decomposition, simple and general pigeonhole principles, then boss. **FinsetBasics** (18 levels) introduces Finset fundamentals—what Finset is, finding deeper elements, empty finsets, range operations, boundaries, variable ranges, reverse direction, bridging between Fin and Finset in both directions, general boundary proofs, non-membership proofs, membership reading, insert idempotence, and nonempty properties.

**FinsetOperations** (25 levels) covers union, intersection, set difference, filter, subset relations, subset monotonicity, equality by antisymmetry, extensionality, idempotency and identity laws, disjoint finsets, commutativity and associativity of union and intersection, lattice notation, targeted simplification, De Morgan's laws, proof by contradiction, complement via sdiff, partition identity, absorption and distributivity laws, then boss. **FinsetImage** (23 levels) starts with first contact with image operations, choosing witnesses, proving non-membership in images, image growth properties, image distribution, union equality, composition, singleton images, intersection subsets and counterexamples, constant function behavior, partial collapse, constant image equality, image shrinking properties, and injective preservation.

Continuing with injectivity proofs, image intersection equality, cardinality preservation and its converse, reverse rewriting, counting arguments for injectivity, and the show tactic before boss. **PsetFinset** (14 levels) is a problem set covering compound membership, double complement subsets, filter and union subsets, union complement, nonempty images, injective non-membership, filter image containment, cardinality ranges, image union with set difference, De Morgan subsets, image monotonicity, and non-membership from image. **Cardinality** (24 levels) explores what cardinality is, cardinality of singletons, nonempty bridges, deriving nonempty from cardinality, zero cardinality, insert operations adding one, insert idempotency, concrete cardinality, range cardinality, erase removing one, subset cardinality bounds, card filter inequalities, and inclusion-exclusion principles for disjoint unions and complements.

Continuing with counting, the multiplication principle, universal finsets, subset relationships with universal sets, concrete universal examples, abstract and general pigeonhole principles, and the surjective-to-injective implication before the capstone. **AbstractionLadder** (17 levels) introduces lists and their ordered nature, list membership, append length properties, permutations and permutation chains, permutation length exercises, multisets and their counting, the nodup bridge connecting lists to finsets, converting multisets to finsets, permutations to finsets, relating nodup cardinality to length, contrasting different levels, and permutation nodup coaching.

Now I'm stepping back to review all 10 worlds holistically and identify where enrichment would have the most impact. I'm noticing that **MeetFin** derives the `Fin.eta` result from extensionality, which is a nice structural insight.

However, there's a gap around making the `Fin n ≃ {k : ℕ | k < n}` equivalence more explicit and central. **FinNavigation** handles dual decompositions well and the succAbove unification is elegant, though it lives in PsetFin rather than FinNavigation itself. **FinTuples** covers cons and snoc injectivity thoroughly but doesn't address when different constructions yield identical tuples—like how there's fundamentally only one function from Fin 0. **FinsetOperations** is comprehensive on set algebra but barely touches Boolean algebra or lattice theory structure beyond a notation mention at level 16. **FinsetImage** beautifully develops the injective-to-card-preserving equivalence, though the surjective direction and its cardinality implications remain underdeveloped.

The pigeonhole principle's connection to surjectivity implying injectivity is handled well in **Cardinality**, but there's no bridge to the formal `Fintype.card` definition. **AbstractionLadder** traces List → Multiset → Finset nicely but doesn't explain why all three layers matter in practice.

On the concrete side, **FinsetOperations** leans heavily abstract with variables when it could benefit from explicit computations like {1,2,3} ∪ {2,3,4}. **FinsetImage** strikes a good balance and the image intersection counterexample at level 10 is particularly strong. **Cardinality** mentions the multiplication principle but could develop it further with more worked examples.

I'm noticing that proof strategies are getting named explicitly—the "unfold-to-logic" approach in **FinsetOperations**, "rewrite-to-values" in **FinNavigation**, and "value repackaging" at level 13. What's missing is a named strategy for the "classify + compare" pattern that appears in pigeonhole arguments.

The connections between phases are clear from Phase 1 to Phase 2, but Phase 2 to Phase 3 (Fintype and big operators) feels underdeveloped, and **AbstractionLadder** doesn't have clear pathways into Phase 3 either. On the Lean side, I'm looking at how much tactic depth is being explored.

The `show` tactic arrives quite late in **FinsetImage**, `push_neg` never gets introduced despite being useful for negation-heavy proofs, and `simp only` is mentioned casually but never formally taught. I should write up a comprehensive enrichment review across all 10 worlds and check whether the reviews directory exists first.

### Phase 3: Cardinality and Structure (Worlds 9-13)

---

#### W09: Cardinality (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will be able to compute cardinalities of finsets using the card_* lemma family, and prove inclusion-exclusion for two sets.

**Theorem families**:
- `Finset.card_empty`, `Finset.card_singleton`
- `Finset.card_insert_of_not_mem`, `Finset.card_erase_of_mem`
- `Finset.card_range`
- `Finset.card_mono` (monotonicity: `s ⊆ t -> card s ≤ card t`)
- `Finset.card_union_add_card_inter` (inclusion-exclusion)
- `Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter` (sdiff + inter)
- `Finset.card_product` (|s ×ˢ t| = |s| * |t|)

**Proof-move goals**:
- Compute card of a finset built by empty/singleton/insert
- Use card_mono for subset-based bounds
- Apply inclusion-exclusion: |s ∪ t| + |s ∩ t| = |s| + |t|
- Use card_product for product cardinality
- Prove equality by showing ≤ in both directions (antisymmetry for ℕ)

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Finset.card`
- `NewTheorem Finset.card_empty Finset.card_singleton Finset.card_insert_of_not_mem Finset.card_mono Finset.card_union_add_card_inter Finset.card_product`
- `norm_num` stays disabled — `omega` suffices for all cardinality arithmetic
- `TheoremTab "Card"`

- L10-L12: Comparing (card_erase, card_le_card/subsets, card_filter_le)
- L13-L16: Combining (inclusion-exclusion, disjoint union, complement counting, multiplication principle)
- L17-L18: Finset.univ (card_univ, card_fin, subset_univ)
- L19: Concrete univ verification (Finset.univ for Fin 3 = {0,1,2})
- L20: Abstract pigeonhole for Fin 3 -> Fin 2
- L21: General pigeonhole for Fin (n+2) -> Fin (n+1)
- L22: Capstone — injective implies surjective (image = univ)

  1573→  1573	|---|---|---|
  1574→  1574	| Union | Always | None |
  1575→  1575	| Intersection | Conditionally | $f$ injective |
  1576→  1576

  1578→  1578	using injectivity — the same move from Level 14, now applied in
  1579→  1579	a membership context rather than a cardinality context. This shows
  1580→  1580	that injectivity is a general-purpose tool, not just a cardinality
  1581→  1581	trick.
  1582→  1582	"

  1589→  1589	Level 19
  1590→  1590	
  1591→  1591	Title "Size Preservation Implies Injectivity"
  1592→  1592	
  1593→  1593	Introduction "
  1594→  1594	# The Converse: A Finite-Math Equivalence
  1595→  1595	
  1596→  1596	Level 13 proved that injective functions preserve cardinality:
  1597→  1597	
  1598→  1598	$$f \\text{ injective} \\implies |f(S)| = |S|$$
  1599→  1599	
  1600→  1600	This level addresses the **converse**: if $|f(S)| = |S|$, must $f$
  1601→  1601	be injective on $S$?
  1602→  1602	
  1603→  1603	For finite sets, the answer is **yes**. This is a deep fact about
  1604→  1604	finiteness: if a function doesn't lose any elements (same cardinality),
  1605→  1605	it can't have any collisions (must be injective). In Mathlib:
  1606→  1606	
  1607→  1607	```
  1608→  1608	Finset.card_image_iff : (s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s
  1609→  1609	```
  1610→  1610	
  1611→  1611	`Set.InjOn f ↑s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
  1612→  1612	for all `a ∈ s` and `b ∈ s`, if `f a = f b` then `a = b`.
  1613→  1613	
  1614→  1614	**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality (`hcard`),
  1615→  1615	and two elements `a, b ∈ s` with `f a = f b`, prove `a = b`.
  1616→  1616	
  1617→  1617	Strategy: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation to an
  1618→  1618	injectivity statement using `Finset.card_image_iff`, then apply it.
  1619→  1619	"
  1620→  1620	
  1621→  1621	/-- If card is preserved, equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
  1622→  1622	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s : Finset ℕ)
  1623→  1623	    (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card)
  1624→  1624	    (a b : ℕ) (ha : a ∈ s) (hb : b ∈ s) (heq : f a = f b) :
  1625→  1625	    a = b := by
  1626→  1626	  Hint "The key step: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation
  1627→  1627	  to an injectivity statement.
  1628→  1628	
  1629→  1629	  Use `rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` to transform
  1630→  1630	  `hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card`
  1631→  1631	  into
  1632→  1632	  `hcard : Set.InjOn f ↑s`."
  1633→  1633	  rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard
  1634→  1634	  Hint "Now `hcard : Set.InjOn f ↑s`, which means
  1635→  1635	  `∀ a ∈ s, ∀ b ∈ s, f a = f b → a = b`.
  1636→  1636	
  1637→  1637	  You have `ha : a ∈ s`, `hb : b ∈ s`, and `heq : f a = f b`.
  1638→  1638	  Apply `hcard` to these: `exact hcard ha hb heq`."
  1639→  1639	  exact hcard ha hb heq
  1640→  1640	
  1641→  1641	Conclusion "
  1642→  1642	This is the **fundamental equivalence** of finite-set images:
  1643→  1643	
  1644→  1644	$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
  1645→  1645	
  1646→  1646	The forward direction ($\\Leftarrow$) is Level 13's
  1647→  1647	`card_image_of_injective`. The backward direction ($\\Rightarrow$)
  1648→  1648	is the converse you just used.
  1649→  1649	
  1650→  1650	This equivalence is **unique to finite sets** — for infinite sets,
  1651→  1651	a function can preserve 'size' (cardinality) while not being
  1652→  1652	injective (e.g., $n \\mapsto \\lfloor n/2 \\rfloor$ maps $\\mathbb{N}$
  1653→  1653	onto $\\mathbb{N}$ but is not injective).
  1654→  1654	
  1655→  1655	The equivalence connects several themes:
  1656→  1656	- **Pigeonhole principle** (PsetFin): if you map $n+1$ pigeons
  1657→  1657	  into $n$ holes, two must collide — contrapositive of the
  1658→  1658	  converse direction
  1659→  1659	- **Counting arguments**: size preservation is a test for
  1660→  1660	  injectivity without checking every pair
  1661→  1661	- **Finite type theory**: `Fintype.card_congr` will generalize
  1662→  1662	  this from `Finset` to `Fintype`
  1663→  1663	
  1664→  1664	**Transferable strategy — counting proves injectivity**: When you
  1665→  1665	need to show a function is injective on a finite set, you have
  1666→  1666	two approaches: (1) check every pair of inputs (often tedious),
  1667→  1667	or (2) count the image and show $|f(S)| = |S|$. Approach (2)
  1668→  1668	is often dramatically easier — you'll see it in action next level.
  1669→  1669	
  1670→  1670	In the next level, you'll see this equivalence in action: using
  1671→  1671	cardinality to prove injectivity for a function where checking
  1672→  1672	every pair directly would be tedious. The backward direction
  1673→  1673	($\\Rightarrow$) is especially powerful: it lets you prove
  1674→  1674	injectivity by counting, avoiding the need to check every pair
  1675→  1675	of inputs.
  1676→  1676	
  1677→  1677	You'll use `card_image_iff` again in the Cardinality world,
  1678→  1678	where counting arguments routinely convert between cardinality
  1679→  1679	equations and injectivity statements.
  1680→  1680	"
  1681→  1681	
  1682→  1682	/-- `Finset.card_image_iff` states that
  1683→  1683	`(s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s`.
  1684→  1684	
  1685→  1685	The image of a finset preserves cardinality if and only if the
  1686→  1686	function is injective on the set.
  1687→  1687	
  1688→  1688	## Syntax
  1689→  1689	```
  1690→  1690	rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard  -- convert card eq to injectivity
  1691→  1691	```
  1692→  1692	
  1693→  1693	## When to use it
  1694→  1694	When you need to convert between a cardinality equation and an
  1695→  1695	injectivity statement on a finset.
  1696→  1696	-/
  1697→  1697	TheoremDoc Finset.card_image_iff as "Finset.card_image_iff" in "Finset"
  1698→  1698	
  1699→  1699	/-- `Set.InjOn f s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
  1700→  1700	for all `a ∈ s` and `b ∈ s`, `f a = f b → a = b`.
  1701→  1701	
  1702→  1702	## Definition
  1703→  1703	```
  1704→  1704	Set.InjOn f s := ∀ a ∈ s, ∀ b ∈ s, f a = f b → a = b
  1705→  1705	```
  1706→  1706	
  1707→  1707	## Relationship to `Function.Injective`
  1708→  1708	- `Function.Injective f` is global: for ALL `a b`.
  1709→  1709	- `Set.InjOn f s` is local: only for `a b ∈ s`.
  1710→  1710	- Every injective function is injective on any set.
  1711→  1711	-/
  1712→  1712	DefinitionDoc Set.InjOn as "Set.InjOn"
  1713→  1713	
  1714→  1714	NewDefinition Set.InjOn
  1715→  1715	NewTheorem Finset.card_image_iff

  1723→  1723	
  1724→  1724	Title "Reverse Rewriting with ←"
  1725→  1725	
  1726→  1726	Introduction "
  1727→  1727	# The `←` Modifier for `rw`
  1728→  1728	
  1729→  1729	So far, every `rw` you've used rewrites **left to right**: if
  1730→  1730	`h : A = B`, then `rw [h]` replaces `A` with `B` in the goal.
  1731→  1731	
  1732→  1732	But sometimes you need the **reverse direction**: replace `B`
  1733→  1733	with `A`. The `←` modifier does this:
  1734→  1734	
  1735→  1735	- `rw [h]` — rewrites left to right: replaces `A` with `B`
  1736→  1736	- `rw [← h]` — rewrites right to left: replaces `B` with `A`
  1737→  1737	
  1738→  1738	Type `←` as `\\l` (backslash-l).
  1739→  1739	
  1740→  1740	**Why this matters**: Many Mathlib lemmas are stated as
  1741→  1741	`A ↔ B`, but your goal matches `B` and you need to convert
  1742→  1742	it to `A`. For example, `Finset.card_image_iff` states:
  1743→  1743	```
  1744→  1744	(s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s
  1745→  1745	```
  1746→  1746	
  1747→  1747	In Level 18, you used forward `rw` on a *hypothesis*:
  1748→  1748	`rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` converted a cardinality
  1749→  1749	equation (left side) into an injectivity statement (right side).
  1750→  1750	
  1751→  1751	Now you'll use `← rw` on the *goal*: if your goal is
  1752→  1752	`Set.InjOn f ↑s` (the right side), then
  1753→  1753	`rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` converts it to
  1754→  1754	`(s.image f).card = s.card` (the left side).
  1755→  1755	
  1756→  1756	**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality, prove
  1757→  1757	that `f` is injective on `s`. Convert the injectivity goal to a
  1758→  1758	cardinality equation using `← rw`, then close with the hypothesis.
  1759→  1759	"
  1760→  1760	
  1761→  1761	/-- Reverse rewriting converts between equivalent formulations. -/
  1762→  1762	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s : Finset ℕ) (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card) :
  1763→  1763	    Set.InjOn f ↑s := by
  1764→  1764	  Hint "Your goal is `Set.InjOn f ↑s` — the RIGHT side of
  1765→  1765	  `Finset.card_image_iff`. Use `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]`
  1766→  1766	  to convert it to `(s.image f).card = s.card` (the LEFT side)."
  1767→  1767	  rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]
  1768→  1768	  Hint "Now the goal is `(s.image f).card = s.card`, which
  1769→  1769	  matches the hypothesis `hcard` exactly."
  1770→  1770	  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact hcard`"
  1771→  1771	  exact hcard
  1772→  1772	
  1773→  1773	Conclusion "
  1774→  1774	You've learned the `←` modifier for `rw`:
  1775→  1775	
  1776→  1776	- `rw [h]` replaces the LEFT side with the RIGHT side
  1777→  1777	- `rw [← h]` replaces the RIGHT side with the LEFT side
  1778→  1778	
  1779→  1779	This works for both equalities (`=`) and biconditionals (`↔`).
  1780→  1780	
  1781→  1781	**Comparison with Level 18**: In Level 18, you used forward `rw`
  1782→  1782	on a hypothesis to convert FROM cardinality TO injectivity. Here
  1783→  1783	you used reverse `rw` on the goal to convert FROM injectivity
  1784→  1784	TO cardinality. Same lemma, opposite direction, different target
  1785→  1785	(hypothesis vs. goal). The `←` gives you full flexibility.
  1786→  1786	
  1787→  1787	The `←` modifier is essential when:
  1788→  1788	- A lemma is stated as `A ↔ B` but your goal matches `B`
  1789→  1789	  (you want to convert to `A`)
  1790→  1790	- A lemma is stated as `A = B` but your goal contains `B`
  1791→  1791	  (you want to go back to `A`)
  1792→  1792	
  1793→  1793	In the next level, you'll see `← rw` used as a proof technique:
  1794→  1794	converting an injectivity goal into a cardinality equation that
  1795→  1795	Lean can verify computationally.
  1796→  1796	"

  1800→  1800	import Game.Metadata
  1801→  1801	
  1802→  1802	World "FinsetImage"
  1803→  1803	Level 21
  1804→  1804	
  1805→  1805	Title "Counting Proves Injectivity"
  1806→  1806	
  1807→  1807	Introduction "
  1808→  1808	# The Payoff: Cardinality as a Proof Technique
  1809→  1809	
  1810→  1810	Level 17 gave you `Finset.card_image_iff`:
  1811→  1811	
  1812→  1812	$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
  1813→  1813	
  1814→  1814	Now you'll use this as a **proof tool**. Consider
  1815→  1815	$f(n) = n \\cdot n + 1$ on $S = \\{0, 1, 2\\}$:
  1816→  1816	
  1817→  1817	| $n$ | $f(n) = n^2 + 1$ |
  1818→  1818	|---|---|
  1819→  1819	| 0 | 1 |
  1820→  1820	| 1 | 2 |
  1821→  1821	| 2 | 5 |
  1822→  1822	
  1823→  1823	The image $\\{1, 2, 5\\}$ has 3 elements, the same as $S$. By
  1824→  1824	`card_image_iff`, this means $f$ is injective on $S$.
  1825→  1825	
  1826→  1826	Proving injectivity directly for a quadratic function requires
  1827→  1827	checking that $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1$ implies $a = b$ for all pairs
  1828→  1828	in $S$ — tedious. But counting the image is immediate: same
  1829→  1829	size means no collisions.
  1830→  1830	
  1831→  1831	**Your task**: Prove that $f$ is injective on $S$ by converting
  1832→  1832	the goal to a cardinality equation, then letting Lean verify
  1833→  1833	the computation.
  1834→  1834	
  1835→  1835	**The `←` modifier**: The `←` arrow before a theorem name tells `rw`
  1836→  1836	to apply the theorem **right-to-left** instead of left-to-right.
  1837→  1837	`Finset.card_image_iff` says `card = card ↔ InjOn`. Normally `rw`
  1838→  1838	would replace `card = card` with `InjOn`. With `←`, it replaces
  1839→  1839	`InjOn` with `card = card` — exactly what we want here.
  1840→  1840	
  1841→  1841	Strategy:
  1842→  1842	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert the injectivity
  1843→  1843	   goal into a cardinality equation
  1844→  1844	2. `native_decide` — let Lean compute both sides and verify equality
  1845→  1845	"
  1846→  1846	
  1847→  1847	/-- Counting proves injectivity: n ↦ n² + 1 is injective on {0, 1, 2}. -/
  1848→  1848	Statement :
  1849→  1849	    Set.InjOn (fun n : ℕ => n * n + 1) ↑({0, 1, 2} : Finset ℕ) := by
  1850→  1850	  Hint "Convert the injectivity goal into a cardinality equation.
  1851→  1851	  Use `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — the `←` rewrites the goal
  1852→  1852	  from `Set.InjOn ...` to `(s.image f).card = s.card`."
  1853→  1853	  rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]
  1854→  1854	  Hint "The goal is now a concrete cardinality equation. Lean can
  1855→  1855	  compute both sides: the image is `(1, 2, 5)` with 3 elements,
  1856→  1856	  and the input has 3 elements. Use `native_decide` to let Lean
  1857→  1857	  verify this computationally."
  1858→  1858	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `native_decide`."
  1859→  1859	  native_decide
  1860→  1860	
  1861→  1861	Conclusion "
  1862→  1862	You just proved injectivity **without checking a single pair**.
  1863→  1863	Instead of showing $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1 \\implies a = b$ for all
  1864→  1864	$a, b \\in \\{0, 1, 2\\}$, you showed $|f(S)| = |S|$ and let
  1865→  1865	`card_image_iff` do the rest.
  1866→  1866	
  1867→  1867	This is the counting-proves-injectivity technique:
  1868→  1868	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert to cardinality
  1869→  1869	2. `native_decide` (or `decide`) — verify computationally
  1870→  1870	
  1871→  1871	The technique works whenever the domain is a concrete finset.
  1872→  1872	For abstract finsets, you'd need to compute or bound the
  1873→  1873	cardinality by other means.
  1874→  1874	
  1875→  1875	This is the **payoff** of the cardinality arc (Levels 12-18):
  1876→  1876	cardinality isn't just a number — it's a proof technique. Knowing
  1877→  1877	$|f(S)| = |S|$ tells you the function has no collisions, which is
  1878→  1878	the *definition* of injectivity.
  1879→  1879	
  1880→  1880	**Name this strategy**: 'counting proves injectivity.' It appears
  1881→  1881	throughout combinatorics — whenever you can count the image and
  1882→  1882	confirm no elements were lost, you've proved injectivity without
  1883→  1883	examining a single pair. The pattern is always the same:
  1884→  1884	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert to cardinality
  1885→  1885	2. Compute or bound the image size
  1886→  1886	3. Conclude injectivity
  1887→  1887	
  1888→  1888	Next: a practice level for the `show` tactic applied to injectivity
  1889→  1889	proofs, then the boss.
  1890→  1890	"

  1898→  1898	import Game.Metadata
  1899→  1899	
  1900→  1900	World "FinsetImage"
  1901→  1901	Level 22
  1902→  1902	
  1903→  1903	Title "Using show for Injectivity"
  1904→  1904	
  1905→  1905	Introduction "
  1906→  1906	# Restating the Goal with show
  1907→  1907	
  1908→  1908	In FinsetOperations, you learned that `show` lets you restate the
  1909→  1909	current goal in an equivalent form that Lean can verify. This is
  1910→  1910	especially useful for injectivity proofs.
  1911→  1911	
  1912→  1912	When Lean displays `Function.Injective (fun n => n * 2)`, the goal
  1913→  1913	is abstract — you can't directly `intro` the arguments. But
  1914→  1914	`Function.Injective f` unfolds to `∀ a b, f a = f b → a = b`. The
  1915→  1915	`show` tactic lets you restate with the function applied:
  1916→  1916	
  1917→  1917	```
  1918→  1918	show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b
  1919→  1919	```
  1920→  1920	
  1921→  1921	After this, `intro a b h` gives concrete variables and `omega`
  1922→  1922	finishes the arithmetic.
  1923→  1923	
  1924→  1924	**Your task**: Prove that doubling is injective by restating with
  1925→  1925	`show` and then using `intro` and `omega`.
  1926→  1926	"
  1927→  1927	
  1928→  1928	/-- Prove injectivity by restating with show. -/
  1929→  1929	Statement : Function.Injective (fun n : ℕ => n * 2) := by
  1930→  1930	  Hint "The goal is `Function.Injective (fun n => n * 2)`.
  1931→  1931	  This unfolds to `∀ a b, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b`.
  1932→  1932	  Use `show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b` to make
  1933→  1933	  this explicit with the function applied."
  1934→  1934	  show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b
  1935→  1935	  Hint "Now `intro a b h` gives you concrete variables and an
  1936→  1936	  equation `h : a * 2 = b * 2`. Then `omega` solves it."
  1937→  1937	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `intro a b h; omega`."
  1938→  1938	  intro a b h
  1939→  1939	  omega
  1940→  1940	
  1941→  1941	Conclusion "
  1942→  1942	The `show` tactic is useful when:
  1943→  1943	- The goal involves an abstract definition (like `Function.Injective`)
  1944→  1944	- You want to see the definition expanded with specific arguments
  1945→  1945	- The expanded form is easier to work with
  1946→  1946	
  1947→  1947	For injectivity proofs, the pattern is always:
  1948→  1948	1. `show ∀ a b : α, f a = f b → a = b` — expand the definition
  1949→  1949	2. `intro a b h` — introduce the variables and the equality
  1950→  1950	3. Prove `a = b` from `h` (often `omega` or algebraic reasoning)
  1951→  1951	
  1952→  1952	The boss will use this pattern as part of a larger proof.
  1953→  1953	"

  1965→  1965	# Boss: Putting It All Together
  1966→  1966	
  1967→  1967	Consider $f(n) = n + 3$ applied to $S = \\{0, 1, \\ldots, 9\\}$.
  1968→  1968	This level integrates three core move families from the world.

  1560	You proved the **repair**: when $f$ is injective,
  1561	
  1562	$$f(S) \\cap f(T) \\subseteq f(S \\cap T)$$
  1563	
  1564	Combined with Level 8 ($f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$,
  1565	which holds for all $f$), this gives the full distributivity:
  1566	
  1567	$$f \\text{ injective} \\implies f(S \\cap T) = f(S) \\cap f(T)$$
  1568	
  1569	Compare with union, where distributivity is free (no injectivity
  1570	needed). This asymmetry is a fundamental theme:
  1571	
  1572	| Operation | Distributes? | Condition |
  1573	|---|---|---|

  1588	World "FinsetImage"
  1589	Level 19
  1590	
  1591	Title "Size Preservation Implies Injectivity"
  1592	
  1593	Introduction "
  1594	# The Converse: A Finite-Math Equivalence
  1595	
  1596	Level 13 proved that injective functions preserve cardinality:
  1597	
  1598	$$f \\text{ injective} \\implies |f(S)| = |S|$$
  1599	
  1600	This level addresses the **converse**: if $|f(S)| = |S|$, must $f$
  1601	be injective on $S$?
  1602	
  1603	For finite sets, the answer is **yes**. This is a deep fact about
  1604	finiteness: if a function doesn't lose any elements (same cardinality),
  1605	it can't have any collisions (must be injective). In Mathlib:
  1606	
  1607	```
  1608	Finset.card_image_iff : (s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s
  1609	```
  1610	
  1611	`Set.InjOn f ↑s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
  1612	for all `a ∈ s` and `b ∈ s`, if `f a = f b` then `a = b`.
  1613	
  1614	**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality (`hcard`),
  1615	and two elements `a, b ∈ s` with `f a = f b`, prove `a = b`.
  1616	
  1617	Strategy: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation to an
  1618	injectivity statement using `Finset.card_image_iff`, then apply it.
  1619	"
  1620	
  1621	/-- If card is preserved, equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
  1622	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s : Finset ℕ)
  1623	    (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card)
  1624	    (a b : ℕ) (ha : a ∈ s) (hb : b ∈ s) (heq : f a = f b) :
  1625	    a = b := by
  1626	  Hint "The key step: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation
  1627	  to an injectivity statement.
  1628	
  1629	  Use `rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` to transform
  1630	  `hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card`
  1631	  into
  1632	  `hcard : Set.InjOn f ↑s`."
  1633	  rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard
  1634	  Hint "Now `hcard : Set.InjOn f ↑s`, which means
  1635	  `∀ a ∈ s, ∀ b ∈ s, f a = f b → a = b`.
  1636	
  1637	  You have `ha : a ∈ s`, `hb : b ∈ s`, and `heq : f a = f b`.
  1638	  Apply `hcard` to these: `exact hcard ha hb heq`."
  1639	  exact hcard ha hb heq
  1640	
  1641	Conclusion "
  1642	This is the **fundamental equivalence** of finite-set images:
  1643	
  1644	$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
  1645	
  1646	The forward direction ($\\Leftarrow$) is Level 13's
  1647	`card_image_of_injective`. The backward direction ($\\Rightarrow$)
  1648	is the converse you just used.
  1649	
  1650	This equivalence is **unique to finite sets** — for infinite sets,
  1651	a function can preserve 'size' (cardinality) while not being
  1652	injective (e.g., $n \\mapsto \\lfloor n/2 \\rfloor$ maps $\\mathbb{N}$
  1653	onto $\\mathbb{N}$ but is not injective).
  1654	
  1655	The equivalence connects several themes:
  1656	- **Pigeonhole principle** (PsetFin): if you map $n+1$ pigeons
  1657	  into $n$ holes, two must collide — contrapositive of the
  1658	  converse direction
  1659	- **Counting arguments**: size preservation is a test for
  1660	  injectivity without checking every pair
  1661	- **Finite type theory**: `Fintype.card_congr` will generalize
  1662	  this from `Finset` to `Fintype`
  1663	
  1664	**Transferable strategy — counting proves injectivity**: When you
  1665	need to show a function is injective on a finite set, you have
  1666	two approaches: (1) check every pair of inputs (often tedious),
  1667	or (2) count the image and show $|f(S)| = |S|$. Approach (2)
  1668	is often dramatically easier — you'll see it in action next level.
  1669	
  1670	In the next level, you'll see this equivalence in action: using
  1671	cardinality to prove injectivity for a function where checking
  1672	every pair directly would be tedious. The backward direction
  1673	($\\Rightarrow$) is especially powerful: it lets you prove
  1674	injectivity by counting, avoiding the need to check every pair
  1675	of inputs.
  1676	
  1677	You'll use `card_image_iff` again in the Cardinality world,
  1678	where counting arguments routinely convert between cardinality
  1679	equations and injectivity statements.
  1680	"
  1681	
  1682	/-- `Finset.card_image_iff` states that
  1683	`(s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s`.
  1684	
  1685	The image of a finset preserves cardinality if and only if the
  1686	function is injective on the set.
  1687	
  1688	## Syntax
  1689	```
  1690	rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard  -- convert card eq to injectivity
  1691	```
  1692	
  1693	## When to use it
  1694	When you need to convert between a cardinality equation and an
  1695	injectivity statement on a finset.
  1696	-/
  1697	TheoremDoc Finset.card_image_iff as "Finset.card_image_iff" in "Finset"
  1698	
  1699	/-- `Set.InjOn f s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
  1700	for all `a ∈ s` and `b ∈ s`, `f a = f b → a = b`.
  1701	
  1702	## Definition
  1703	```
  1704	Set.InjOn f s := ∀ a ∈ s, ∀ b ∈ s, f a = f b → a = b
  1705	```
  1706	
  1707	## Relationship to `Function.Injective`
  1708	- `Function.Injective f` is global: for ALL `a b`.
  1709	- `Set.InjOn f s` is local: only for `a b ∈ s`.
  1710	- Every injective function is injective on any set.
  1711	-/
  1712	DefinitionDoc Set.InjOn as "Set.InjOn"

  1721	World "FinsetImage"
  1722	Level 20
  1723	
  1724	Title "Reverse Rewriting with ←"
  1725	
  1726	Introduction "
  1727	# The `←` Modifier for `rw`
  1728	
  1729	So far, every `rw` you've used rewrites **left to right**: if
  1730	`h : A = B`, then `rw [h]` replaces `A` with `B` in the goal.
  1731	
  1732	But sometimes you need the **reverse direction**: replace `B`
  1733	with `A`. The `←` modifier does this:
  1734	
  1735	- `rw [h]` — rewrites left to right: replaces `A` with `B`
  1736	- `rw [← h]` — rewrites right to left: replaces `B` with `A`
  1737	
  1738	Type `←` as `\\l` (backslash-l).
  1739	
  1740	**Why this matters**: Many Mathlib lemmas are stated as
  1741	`A ↔ B`, but your goal matches `B` and you need to convert
  1742	it to `A`. For example, `Finset.card_image_iff` states:
  1743	```
  1744	(s.image f).card = s.card ↔ Set.InjOn f ↑s
  1745	```
  1746	
  1747	In Level 18, you used forward `rw` on a *hypothesis*:
  1748	`rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` converted a cardinality
  1749	equation (left side) into an injectivity statement (right side).
  1750	
  1751	Now you'll use `← rw` on the *goal*: if your goal is
  1752	`Set.InjOn f ↑s` (the right side), then
  1753	`rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` converts it to
  1754	`(s.image f).card = s.card` (the left side).
  1755	
  1756	**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality, prove
  1757	that `f` is injective on `s`. Convert the injectivity goal to a
  1758	cardinality equation using `← rw`, then close with the hypothesis.
  1759	"
  1760	
  1761	/-- Reverse rewriting converts between equivalent formulations. -/
  1762	Statement (f : ℕ → ℕ) (s : Finset ℕ) (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card) :
  1763	    Set.InjOn f ↑s := by
  1764	  Hint "Your goal is `Set.InjOn f ↑s` — the RIGHT side of
  1765	  `Finset.card_image_iff`. Use `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]`
  1766	  to convert it to `(s.image f).card = s.card` (the LEFT side)."
  1767	  rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]
  1768	  Hint "Now the goal is `(s.image f).card = s.card`, which
  1769	  matches the hypothesis `hcard` exactly."
  1770	  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact hcard`"
  1771	  exact hcard
  1772	
  1773	Conclusion "
  1774	You've learned the `←` modifier for `rw`:
  1775	
  1776	- `rw [h]` replaces the LEFT side with the RIGHT side
  1777	- `rw [← h]` replaces the RIGHT side with the LEFT side
  1778	
  1779	This works for both equalities (`=`) and biconditionals (`↔`).
  1780	
  1781	**Comparison with Level 18**: In Level 18, you used forward `rw`
  1782	on a hypothesis to convert FROM cardinality TO injectivity. Here
  1783	you used reverse `rw` on the goal to convert FROM injectivity
  1784	TO cardinality. Same lemma, opposite direction, different target
  1785	(hypothesis vs. goal). The `←` gives you full flexibility.
  1786	
  1787	The `←` modifier is essential when:
  1788	- A lemma is stated as `A ↔ B` but your goal matches `B`
  1789	  (you want to convert to `A`)
  1790	- A lemma is stated as `A = B` but your goal contains `B`
  1791	  (you want to go back to `A`)
  1792	
  1793	In the next level, you'll see `← rw` used as a proof technique:
  1794	converting an injectivity goal into a cardinality equation that
  1795	Lean can verify computationally.
  1796	"
  1797

  1800	import Game.Metadata
  1801	
  1802	World "FinsetImage"
  1803	Level 21
  1804	
  1805	Title "Counting Proves Injectivity"
  1806	
  1807	Introduction "
  1808	# The Payoff: Cardinality as a Proof Technique
  1809	
  1810	Level 17 gave you `Finset.card_image_iff`:
  1811	
  1812	$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
  1813	
  1814	Now you'll use this as a **proof tool**. Consider
  1815	$f(n) = n \\cdot n + 1$ on $S = \\{0, 1, 2\\}$:
  1816	
  1817	| $n$ | $f(n) = n^2 + 1$ |
  1818	|---|---|
  1819	| 0 | 1 |
  1820	| 1 | 2 |
  1821	| 2 | 5 |
  1822	
  1823	The image $\\{1, 2, 5\\}$ has 3 elements, the same as $S$. By
  1824	`card_image_iff`, this means $f$ is injective on $S$.
  1825	
  1826	Proving injectivity directly for a quadratic function requires
  1827	checking that $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1$ implies $a = b$ for all pairs
  1828	in $S$ — tedious. But counting the image is immediate: same
  1829	size means no collisions.
  1830	
  1831	**Your task**: Prove that $f$ is injective on $S$ by converting
  1832	the goal to a cardinality equation, then letting Lean verify
  1833	the computation.
  1834	
  1835	**The `←` modifier**: The `←` arrow before a theorem name tells `rw`
  1836	to apply the theorem **right-to-left** instead of left-to-right.
  1837	`Finset.card_image_iff` says `card = card ↔ InjOn`. Normally `rw`
  1838	would replace `card = card` with `InjOn`. With `←`, it replaces
  1839	`InjOn` with `card = card` — exactly what we want here.
  1840	
  1841	Strategy:
  1842	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert the injectivity
  1843	   goal into a cardinality equation
  1844	2. `native_decide` — let Lean compute both sides and verify equality
  1845	"
  1846	
  1847	/-- Counting proves injectivity: n ↦ n² + 1 is injective on {0, 1, 2}. -/
  1848	Statement :
  1849	    Set.InjOn (fun n : ℕ => n * n + 1) ↑({0, 1, 2} : Finset ℕ) := by
  1850	  Hint "Convert the injectivity goal into a cardinality equation.
  1851	  Use `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — the `←` rewrites the goal
  1852	  from `Set.InjOn ...` to `(s.image f).card = s.card`."
  1853	  rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]
  1854	  Hint "The goal is now a concrete cardinality equation. Lean can
  1855	  compute both sides: the image is `(1, 2, 5)` with 3 elements,
  1856	  and the input has 3 elements. Use `native_decide` to let Lean
  1857	  verify this computationally."
  1858	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `native_decide`."
  1859	  native_decide
  1860	
  1861	Conclusion "
  1862	You just proved injectivity **without checking a single pair**.
  1863	Instead of showing $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1 \\implies a = b$ for all
  1864	$a, b \\in \\{0, 1, 2\\}$, you showed $|f(S)| = |S|$ and let
  1865	`card_image_iff` do the rest.
  1866	
  1867	This is the counting-proves-injectivity technique:
  1868	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert to cardinality
  1869	2. `native_decide` (or `decide`) — verify computationally
  1870	
  1871	The technique works whenever the domain is a concrete finset.
  1872	For abstract finsets, you'd need to compute or bound the
  1873	cardinality by other means.
  1874	
  1875	This is the **payoff** of the cardinality arc (Levels 12-18):
  1876	cardinality isn't just a number — it's a proof technique. Knowing
  1877	$|f(S)| = |S|$ tells you the function has no collisions, which is
  1878	the *definition* of injectivity.
  1879	
  1880	**Name this strategy**: 'counting proves injectivity.' It appears
  1881	throughout combinatorics — whenever you can count the image and
  1882	confirm no elements were lost, you've proved injectivity without
  1883	examining a single pair. The pattern is always the same:
  1884	1. `rw [← Finset.card_image_iff]` — convert to cardinality
  1885	2. Compute or bound the image size
  1886	3. Conclude injectivity
  1887	
  1888	Next: a practice level for the `show` tactic applied to injectivity
  1889	proofs, then the boss.
  1890	"
  1891

  1900	World "FinsetImage"
  1901	Level 22
  1902	
  1903	Title "Using show for Injectivity"
  1904	
  1905	Introduction "
  1906	# Restating the Goal with show
  1907	
  1908	In FinsetOperations, you learned that `show` lets you restate the
  1909	current goal in an equivalent form that Lean can verify. This is
  1910	especially useful for injectivity proofs.
  1911	
  1912	When Lean displays `Function.Injective (fun n => n * 2)`, the goal
  1913	is abstract — you can't directly `intro` the arguments. But
  1914	`Function.Injective f` unfolds to `∀ a b, f a = f b → a = b`. The
  1915	`show` tactic lets you restate with the function applied:
  1916	
  1917	```
  1918	show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b
  1919	```
  1920	
  1921	After this, `intro a b h` gives concrete variables and `omega`
  1922	finishes the arithmetic.
  1923	
  1924	**Your task**: Prove that doubling is injective by restating with
  1925	`show` and then using `intro` and `omega`.
  1926	"
  1927	
  1928	/-- Prove injectivity by restating with show. -/
  1929	Statement : Function.Injective (fun n : ℕ => n * 2) := by
  1930	  Hint "The goal is `Function.Injective (fun n => n * 2)`.
  1931	  This unfolds to `∀ a b, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b`.
  1932	  Use `show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b` to make
  1933	  this explicit with the function applied."
  1934	  show ∀ a b : ℕ, a * 2 = b * 2 → a = b
  1935	  Hint "Now `intro a b h` gives you concrete variables and an
  1936	  equation `h : a * 2 = b * 2`. Then `omega` solves it."
  1937	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `intro a b h; omega`."
  1938	  intro a b h
  1939	  omega
  1940	
  1941	Conclusion "
  1942	The `show` tactic is useful when:
  1943	- The goal involves an abstract definition (like `Function.Injective`)
  1944	- You want to see the definition expanded with specific arguments
  1945	- The expanded form is easier to work with
  1946	
  1947	For injectivity proofs, the pattern is always:
  1948	1. `show ∀ a b : α, f a = f b → a = b` — expand the definition
  1949	2. `intro a b h` — introduce the variables and the equality
  1950	3. Prove `a = b` from `h` (often `omega` or algebraic reasoning)
  1951	
  1952	The boss will use this pattern as part of a larger proof.
  1953	"

  1961	
  1962	Title "Boss: Image Membership, Bounds, and Injectivity"
  1963	
  1964	Introduction "
  1965	# Boss: Putting It All Together
  1966	
  1967	Consider $f(n) = n + 3$ applied to $S = \\{0, 1, \\ldots, 9\\}$.
  1968	This level integrates three core move families from the world.
  1969	
  1970	**Part 1** — Forward membership (Levels 1-2):
  1971	Prove $12 \\in f(S)$ by providing the witness $9$.
  1972	
  1973	**Part 2** — Subset bound via backward extraction (Levels 3-8):
  1974	Prove $f(S) \\subseteq \\{0, \\ldots, 12\\}$. Every element
  1975	$n + 3$ where $n < 10$ satisfies $n + 3 < 13$.
  1976	
  1977	**Part 3** — Cardinality upper bound (Level 12):
  1978	The image can only shrink: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$.
  1979	
  1980	**Part 4** — Exact cardinality via injectivity (Levels 13-14):
  1981	Prove $|f(S)| = |S|$ by proving that $n \\mapsto n+3$ is injective.
  1982	
  1983	No new moves — only moves seeded in Levels 1-21.
  1984	"
  1985	
  1986	/-- Forward membership, subset bound, cardinality bounds, and injectivity. -/
  1987	Statement :
  1988	    (12 : ℕ) ∈ (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) ∧
  1989	    (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) ⊆ Finset.range 13 ∧
  1990	    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card ≤
  1991	      (Finset.range 10).card ∧
  1992	    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card =
  1993	      (Finset.range 10).card := by
  1994	  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into Part 1 and the remaining parts."
  1995	  constructor
  1996	  -- Part 1: 12 ∈ image (· + 3) (range 10)
  1997	  · Hint "Forward membership: `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, provide the witness.
  1998	    Solve: $n + 3 = 12$ gives $n = 9$."
  1999	    rw [Finset.mem_image]

1000→  1000	`(s ∪ t).card = s.card + t.card`.
  1001→  1001	
  1002→  1002	## Syntax
  1003→  1003	```
  1004→  1004	have hd := Finset.card_union_of_disjoint h
  1005→  1005	```
  1006→  1006	
  1007→  1007	## When to use it
  1008→  1008	When `s` and `t` are known to be disjoint and you need the cardinality
  1009→  1009	of their union.
  1010→  1010	
  1011→  1011	## Where disjointness comes from
  1012→  1012	Common sources of `Disjoint s t`:
  1013→  1013	- `sdiff_disjoint : Disjoint (s \\ t) t`
  1014→  1014	- Explicitly constructed disjoint partitions
  1015→  1015	- `Finset.disjoint_filter_filter` for complementary predicates
  1016→  1016	-/

  1023→  1023	import Game.Metadata
  1024→  1024	
  1025→  1025	World "Cardinality"
  1026→  1026	Level 15
  1027→  1027	
  1028→  1028	Title "Complement Counting"
  1029→  1029	
  1030→  1030	Introduction "
  1031→  1031	# Counting by Splitting
  1032→  1032	
  1033→  1033	Every finset `s` splits into two disjoint pieces relative to any
  1034→  1034	other finset `t`:
  1035→  1035	- The elements in both: $s \\cap t$
  1036→  1036	- The elements in `s` but not `t`: $s \\setminus t$
  1037→  1037	
  1038→  1038	Since these two pieces are disjoint and their union is `s`, their
  1039→  1039	cardinalities add up to $|s|$:
  1040→  1040	
  1041→  1041	$$|s \\setminus t| + |s \\cap t| = |s|$$
  1042→  1042	
  1043→  1043	In Lean:
  1044→  1044	```
  1045→  1045	Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t :
  1046→  1046	    (s \\ t).card + (s ∩ t).card = s.card
  1047→  1047	```
  1048→  1048	
  1049→  1049	This is **complement counting**: if you know two of the three
  1050→  1050	quantities ($|s|$, $|s \\setminus t|$, $|s \\cap t|$), you can compute
  1051→  1051	the third.
  1052→  1052	
  1053→  1053	**Your task**: Given $|s| = 8$ and $|s \\cap t| = 3$, compute
  1054→  1054	$|s \\setminus t|$.
  1055→  1055	"
  1056→  1056	
  1057→  1057	/-- Compute the set difference cardinality by complement counting. -/
  1058→  1058	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (hs : s.card = 8)
  1059→  1059	    (hi : (s ∩ t).card = 3) : (s \ t).card = 5 := by
  1060→  1060	  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` to get the
  1061→  1061	  splitting identity. Then use `omega` with the given sizes."
  1062→  1062	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try
  1063→  1063	  `have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` and then `omega`."
  1064→  1064	  have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t
  1065→  1065	  omega
  1066→  1066	
  1067→  1067	Conclusion "
  1068→  1068	Complement counting is the 'sibling' of inclusion-exclusion:
  1069→  1069	- **Inclusion-exclusion**: $|s \\cup t| + |s \\cap t| = |s| + |t|$
  1070→  1070	  — relates the union to the sum of sizes
  1071→  1071	- **Complement counting**: $|s \\setminus t| + |s \\cap t| = |s|$
  1072→  1072	  — relates the difference to the whole
  1073→  1073	
  1074→  1074	Both express the idea that counting should be additive on disjoint
  1075→  1075	pieces. In fact, complement counting IS a special case of disjoint
  1076→  1076	union: $s = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\cap t)$ is a disjoint
  1077→  1077	decomposition.
  1078→  1078	
  1079→  1079	In plain math, this is the strategy 'count the complement':
  1080→  1080	if you want $|s \\setminus t|$, compute $|s| - |s \\cap t|$.
  1081→  1081	"
  1082→  1082	
  1083→  1083	/-- `Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` states that
  1084→  1084	`(s \\ t).card + (s ∩ t).card = s.card`.
  1085→  1085	
  1086→  1086	## Syntax
  1087→  1087	```
  1088→  1088	have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t
  1089→  1089	```
  1090→  1090	
  1091→  1091	## When to use it
  1092→  1092	When you need to relate `(s \\ t).card` to `s.card` and
  1093→  1093	`(s ∩ t).card`. This is complement counting: the set difference
  1094→  1094	and the intersection partition `s`.
  1095→  1095	
  1096→  1096	## Related
  1097→  1097	- `Finset.card_sdiff_of_subset h` — when `s ⊆ t`:
  1098→  1098	  `(t \\ s).card = t.card - s.card`
  1099→  1099	-/
  1100→  1100	TheoremDoc Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter as "Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter" in "Card"

  1103→  1103	NewTheorem Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter

  1106→  1106	import Game.Metadata
  1107→  1107	
  1108→  1108	World "Cardinality"
  1109→  1109	Level 16
  1110→  1110	
  1111→  1111	Title "The Multiplication Principle"
  1112→  1112	
  1113→  1113	Introduction "
  1114→  1114	# Counting Pairs
  1115→  1115	
  1116→  1116	If you can choose one item from $s$ and one from $t$, how many
  1117→  1117	combined choices do you have? The answer is $|s| \\times |t|$.
  1118→  1118	
  1119→  1119	In Lean, `s ×ˢ t` is the **product** of two finsets — the set of
  1120→  1120	all pairs $(a, b)$ with $a \\in s$ and $b \\in t$:
  1121→  1121	
  1122→  1122	$$|s \\times^s t| = |s| \\cdot |t|$$
  1123→  1123	
  1124→  1124	```
  1125→  1125	Finset.card_product s t : (s ×ˢ t).card = s.card * t.card
  1126→  1126	```
  1127→  1127	
  1128→  1128	This is the **multiplication principle** — the second most important
  1129→  1129	counting identity after inclusion-exclusion.
  1130→  1130	
  1131→  1131	**Your task**: Given two finsets of known sizes, compute the size
  1132→  1132	of their product.
  1133→  1133	"
  1134→  1134	
  1135→  1135	/-- Compute the product cardinality from the individual sizes. -/
  1136→  1136	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (hs : s.card = 4) (ht : t.card = 3) :
  1137→  1137	    (s ×ˢ t).card = 12 := by
  1138→  1138	  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_product` to rewrite the left side,
  1139→  1139	  then substitute the known sizes."
  1140→  1140	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]`.
  1141→  1141	  This rewrites the goal step by step:
  1142→  1142	  `(s ×ˢ t).card` → `s.card * t.card` → `4 * t.card` → `4 * 3`."
  1143→  1143	  rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]
  1144→  1144	
  1145→  1145	Conclusion "
  1146→  1146	The multiplication principle: $|s \\times^s t| = |s| \\cdot |t|$.
  1147→  1147	
  1148→  1148	Unlike the additive card lemmas (`have` + `omega`), the product lemma
  1149→  1149	involves multiplication, so we use `rw` instead:
  1150→  1150	`rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]` directly computes the result.
  1151→  1151	
  1152→  1152	**Why 'product'?** The notation `s ×ˢ t` is the Cartesian product
  1153→  1153	specialized to finsets. Just as `Fin m × Fin n` has `m * n` elements
  1154→  1154	(which you could verify by exhaustive case analysis from Phase 1),
  1155→  1155	`s ×ˢ t` has `s.card * t.card` elements.
  1156→  1156	
  1157→  1157	The multiplication principle combines with inclusion-exclusion:
  1158→  1158	- **Addition**: $|A \\cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \\cap B|$ (for overlapping choices)
  1159→  1159	- **Multiplication**: $|A \\times B| = |A| \\cdot |B|$ (for independent choices)
  1160→  1160	
  1161→  1161	Together they handle the two fundamental counting scenarios:
  1162→  1162	'choose from A or B' vs. 'choose from A and B.'
  1163→  1163	"
  1164→  1164	
  1165→  1165	/-- `Finset.card_product s t` states that
  1166→  1166	`(s ×ˢ t).card = s.card * t.card`.
  1167→  1167	
  1168→  1168	This is the **multiplication principle**: the number of pairs
  1169→  1169	is the product of the individual counts.
  1170→  1170	
  1171→  1171	## Syntax
  1172→  1172	```
  1173→  1173	have h := Finset.card_product s t
  1174→  1174	```
  1175→  1175	
  1176→  1176	## When to use it
  1177→  1177	When the goal involves the cardinality of a product `s ×ˢ t`.
  1178→  1178	
  1179→  1179	## Notation
  1180→  1180	`s ×ˢ t` is typed as `\x\^s` or `\times\^s`.
  1181→  1181	-/
  1182→  1182	TheoremDoc Finset.card_product as "Finset.card_product" in "Card"
  1183→  1183	
  1184→  1184	TheoremTab "Card"
  1185→  1185	NewTheorem Finset.card_product

  1188→  1188	import Game.Metadata

  1190→  1190	World "Cardinality"
  1191→  1191	Level 17
  1192→  1192	
  1193→  1193	Title "The Universal Finset"
  1194→  1194	
  1195→  1195	Introduction "
  1196→  1196	# Finset.univ: All Elements of a Finite Type
  1197→  1197	
  1198→  1198	So far, your finsets have been built by hand: `insert`, `singleton`,
  1199→  1199	`range`, `filter`, `image`. But for a finite type like `Fin n`, there's
  1200→  1200	a finset that contains **all** elements: `Finset.univ`.
  1201→  1201	
  1202→  1202	`Finset.univ : Finset (Fin n)` — the finset of every element of `Fin n`.
  1203→  1203	
  1204→  1204	Two key facts connect `univ` to cardinality:
  1205→  1205	
  1206→  1206	- `Finset.card_univ : (Finset.univ : Finset α).card = Fintype.card α`
  1207→  1207	  — the size of `univ` equals the type's cardinality
  1208→  1208	- `Fintype.card_fin n : Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`
  1209→  1209	  — the type `Fin n` has exactly `n` elements
  1210→  1210	
  1211→  1211	Chaining these: `(Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 5)).card = 5`.
  1212→  1212	
  1213→  1213	**Your task**: Prove that `Finset.univ` for `Fin 5` has exactly 5 elements.
  1214→  1214	"
  1215→  1215	
  1216→  1216	/-- The universal finset of Fin 5 has 5 elements. -/
  1217→  1217	Statement : (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 5)).card = 5 := by
  1218→  1218	  Hint "Use `Finset.card_univ` to convert the univ cardinality to
  1219→  1219	  `Fintype.card (Fin 5)`, then `Fintype.card_fin` to get `5`."
  1220→  1220	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin]`."
  1221→  1221	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin]
  1222→  1222	
  1223→  1223	Conclusion "
  1224→  1224	`Finset.univ` is the finset counterpart of 'the entire type.'
  1225→  1225	
  1226→  1226	| Tool | What it says |
  1227→  1227	|---|---|
  1228→  1228	| `Finset.univ` | The finset of all elements of a finite type |
  1229→  1229	| `Finset.card_univ` | `(Finset.univ).card = Fintype.card α` |
  1230→  1230	| `Fintype.card_fin n` | `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` |
  1231→  1231	
  1232→  1232	Together: `(Finset.univ : Finset (Fin n)).card = n`. This connects
  1233→  1233	the finset world (where you count elements of a set) to the type world
  1234→  1234	(where you count elements of a type).
  1235→  1235	
  1236→  1236	The next level introduces `Finset.subset_univ` — the fact that every
  1237→  1237	finset of a finite type is contained in `univ`. Combined with
  1238→  1238	`card_le_card`, this gives cardinality upper bounds.
  1239→  1239	"
  1240→  1240	
  1241→  1241	/-- `Finset.univ` is the finset of all elements of a finite type.
  1242→  1242	
  1243→  1243	For `Fin n`, `Finset.univ` contains all `n` elements.
  1244→  1244	
  1245→  1245	## Example
  1246→  1246	```
  1247→  1247	Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3) -- contains 0, 1, 2
  1248→  1248	```
  1249→  1249	-/
  1250→  1250	DefinitionDoc Finset.univ as "Finset.univ"
  1251→  1251	
  1252→  1252	NewDefinition Finset.univ
  1253→  1253	
  1254→  1254	/-- `Finset.card_univ` states that `(Finset.univ : Finset α).card = Fintype.card α`:
  1255→  1255	the cardinality of the universal finset equals the type's cardinality. -/
  1256→  1256	TheoremDoc Finset.card_univ as "Finset.card_univ" in "Card"
  1257→  1257	
  1258→  1258	/-- `Fintype.card_fin n` states that `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`:
  1259→  1259	the type `Fin n` has exactly `n` elements. -/
  1260→  1260	TheoremDoc Fintype.card_fin as "Fintype.card_fin" in "Card"
  1261→  1261	
  1262→  1262	/-- `Finset.mem_univ x` states that every element `x` of a finite type
  1263→  1263	belongs to `Finset.univ`. This is always true — `univ` contains everything. -/
  1264→  1264	TheoremDoc Finset.mem_univ as "Finset.mem_univ" in "Card"
  1265→  1265	
  1266→  1266	NewTheorem Finset.card_univ Fintype.card_fin Finset.mem_univ
  1267→  1267	
  1268→  1268	TheoremTab "Card"
  1269→  1269	
  1270→  1270	DisabledTactic trivial «decide» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
  1271→  1271	DisabledTheorem Finset.card_le_univ Finset.eq_univ_of_card
  1272→  1272	import Game.Metadata
  1273→  1273	
  1274→  1274	World "Cardinality"
  1275→  1275	Level 18
  1276→  1276	
  1277→  1277	Title "Everything Lives in Univ"
  1278→  1278	
  1279→  1279	Introduction "
  1280→  1280	# Bounding Cardinality via Univ
  1281→  1281	
  1282→  1282	Every finset of a finite type is a subset of `Finset.univ`:
  1283→  1283	
  1284→  1284	```
  1285→  1285	Finset.subset_univ s : s ⊆ Finset.univ
  1286→  1286	```
  1287→  1287	
  1288→  1288	This is the finset version of 'every element belongs to the universe.'
  1289→  1289	
  1290→  1290	Combined with `card_le_card` (subsets have smaller cardinality), this
  1291→  1291	gives an upper bound on any finset's size:
  1292→  1292	
  1293→  1293	$$|s| \\leq |\\text{Fin}\\;n| = n$$
  1294→  1294	
  1295→  1295	The proof pattern:
  1296→  1296	1. `Finset.subset_univ s` gives `s ⊆ Finset.univ`
  1297→  1297	2. `Finset.card_le_card` converts subset to `s.card ≤ Finset.univ.card`
  1298→  1298	3. `Finset.card_univ` + `Fintype.card_fin` evaluates `Finset.univ.card`
  1299→  1299	
  1300→  1300	**Your task**: Prove that any finset of `Fin 4` elements has at most
  1301→  1301	4 elements.
  1302→  1302	"
  1303→  1303	
  1304→  1304	/-- Any finset of Fin 4 has at most 4 elements. -/
  1305→  1305	Statement (s : Finset (Fin 4)) : s.card ≤ 4 := by
  1306→  1306	  Hint "Use `Finset.subset_univ s` to get the subset relation,
  1307→  1307	  then `Finset.card_le_card` to convert it to a cardinality bound."
  1308→  1308	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
  1309→  1309	  `have h1 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ s)`
  1310→  1310	  `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1`
  1311→  1311	  `exact h1`"
  1312→  1312	  have h1 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ s)
  1313→  1313	  Hint "Now `h1 : s.card ≤ Finset.univ.card`. Simplify the right side
  1314→  1314	  using `card_univ` and `card_fin`."
  1315→  1315	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1
  1316→  1316	  Hint "Now `h1 : s.card ≤ 4`. This is exactly the goal."
  1317→  1317	  exact h1
  1318→  1318	
  1319→  1319	Conclusion "
  1320→  1320	You composed three tools:
  1321→  1321	1. `subset_univ` — every finset is a subset of the universe
  1322→  1322	2. `card_le_card` — subsets have smaller cardinality
  1323→  1323	3. `card_univ` + `card_fin` — the universe has `n` elements
  1324→  1324	
  1325→  1325	This gives the fundamental upper bound: no finset of `Fin n` elements
  1326→  1326	can have more than `n` elements. This may seem obvious, but it's the
  1327→  1327	key ingredient for the next level: proving the pigeonhole principle
  1328→  1328	via cardinality.
  1329→  1329	"
  1330→  1330	
  1331→  1331	/-- `Finset.subset_univ s` states that every finset `s` is a subset of `Finset.univ`.
  1332→  1332	
  1333→  1333	This is the finset version of 'every element belongs to the universe.'
  1334→  1334	-/
  1335→  1335	TheoremDoc Finset.subset_univ as "Finset.subset_univ" in "Finset"
  1336→  1336

  1350→  1350	Introduction "
  1351→  1351	# Grounding Finset.univ in Concrete Terms
  1352→  1352	
  1353→  1353	Level 17 introduced `Finset.univ` as the finset of ALL elements of a
  1354→  1354	finite type. Level 18 showed that everything is a subset of `univ`.
  1355→  1355	But what does `univ` actually **contain**?
  1356→  1356	
  1357→  1357	For `Fin 3`, `Finset.univ` should be exactly `{0, 1, 2}` — the three
  1358→  1358	elements of the type. Let's verify this computationally.
  1359→  1359	
  1360→  1360	You learned `native_decide` in FinsetImage — it lets Lean compute
  1361→  1361	both sides of a decidable equality and check they match. Since both
  1362→  1362	`Finset.univ` and `{0, 1, 2}` are concrete computable finsets, Lean
  1363→  1363	can verify their equality directly.
  1364→  1364	
  1365→  1365	**Your task**: Prove that `Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)` equals
  1366→  1366	the literal finset `{0, 1, 2}`.
  1367→  1367	"
  1368→  1368	
  1369→  1369	/-- The universal finset of Fin 3 is exactly {0, 1, 2}. -/
  1370→  1370	Statement : (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)) = {0, 1, 2} := by
  1371→  1371	  Hint "Both sides are concrete, computable finsets. Use `native_decide`
  1372→  1372	  to let Lean verify the equality computationally."
  1373→  1373	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `native_decide`."
  1374→  1374	  native_decide
  1375→  1375

  1378→  1378	
  1379→  1379	$$\\text{Finset.univ} : \\text{Finset}\\;(\\text{Fin}\\;3) = \\{0, 1, 2\\}$$
  1380→  1380	
  1381→  1381	This is the concrete grounding of the abstract tool: `univ` for `Fin n`
  1382→  1382	contains exactly the elements $0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. When Level 17 proved
  1383→  1383	`(Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 5)).card = 5`, the 5 came from *these*

  1401→  1401	World "Cardinality"

  1403→  1403	
  1404→  1404	Title "Abstract Pigeonhole via Cardinality"
  1405→  1405	
  1406→  1406	Introduction "
  1407→  1407	# Pigeonhole Without Case Analysis
  1408→  1408	
  1409→  1409	In PsetFin, you proved the pigeonhole principle for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`
  1410→  1410	by exhaustive case analysis — 6 branches, each checked by hand.
  1411→  1411	The conclusion promised: 'cardinality tools prove pigeonhole for
  1412→  1412	any n in a few lines.' Time to deliver.
  1413→  1413	
  1414→  1414	The cardinality argument is beautifully simple:
  1415→  1415	
  1416→  1416	1. If `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` were injective, then by
  1417→  1417	   `card_image_of_injective`, the image of ALL `Fin 3` elements
  1418→  1418	   under `f` would have `3` elements.
  1419→  1419	
  1420→  1420	2. But the image lives inside `Fin 2`, which only has `2` elements.
  1421→  1421	   By `card_le_card` + `subset_univ`, the image has
  1422→  1422	   at most `2` elements.
  1423→  1423	
  1424→  1424	3. So `3 <= 2` — contradiction.
  1425→  1425	
  1426→  1426	No case analysis. No classifying outputs. Just counting.
  1427→  1427	
  1428→  1428	All three tools were introduced in the previous two levels:
  1429→  1429	`Finset.univ`, `card_univ`, `card_fin`, and `subset_univ`. Now you
  1430→  1430	combine them with `card_image_of_injective` and `card_le_card` for
  1431→  1431	the full pigeonhole argument.
  1432→  1432	"
  1433→  1433	
  1434→  1434	/-- No injective function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 exists. -/
  1435→  1435	Statement (f : Fin 3 → Fin 2) : ¬ Function.Injective f := by
  1436→  1436	  Hint "Assume for contradiction that `f` is injective.
  1437→  1437	  Use `intro hinj`."
  1438→  1438	  intro hinj
  1439→  1439	  Hint "Use `card_image_of_injective` to get the size of the image.
  1440→  1440	  `have h1 := Finset.card_image_of_injective (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)) hinj`
  1441→  1441	  gives: `(Finset.univ.image f).card = Finset.univ.card`."
  1442→  1442	  Hint (hidden := true) "Then use `Finset.card_univ` and `Fintype.card_fin`
  1443→  1443	  to simplify: `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1`.
  1444→  1444	  This gives `(univ.image f).card = 3`."
  1445→  1445	  have h1 := Finset.card_image_of_injective (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)) hinj
  1446→  1446	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1
  1447→  1447	  Hint "Now bound the image size. The image `univ.image f` is a
  1448→  1448	  subset of `univ : Finset (Fin 2)` (everything lives in the target).
  1449→  1449	  Use `card_le_card` with `subset_univ`."
  1450→  1450	  Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ ((Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)).image f))`
  1451→  1451	  `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h2`
  1452→  1452	  gives `(univ.image f).card <= 2`."
  1453→  1453	  have h2 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ ((Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3)).image f))
  1454→  1454	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h2
  1455→  1455	  Hint "Now `h1` says the image has 3 elements, `h2` says it
  1456→  1456	  has at most 2. `omega` sees the contradiction."
  1457→  1457	  omega
  1458→  1458	
  1459→  1459	Conclusion "
  1460→  1460	The entire proof in 4 lines:
  1461→  1461	1. `card_image_of_injective` + injectivity gives image size = source size = 3
  1462→  1462	2. `card_le_card` + `subset_univ` gives image size <= target size = 2
  1463→  1463	3. `omega`: 3 <= 2 is false
  1464→  1464	
  1465→  1465	Compare this to the PsetFin proof: 99 lines of case analysis
  1466→  1466	with 6 branches. The cardinality argument works for ANY `n`:
  1467→  1467	no injective function from `Fin (n+2)` to `Fin (n+1)` exists,
  1468→  1468	because the image would need `n+2` elements but the target only
  1469→  1469	has `n+1`. No enumeration, no branches — just arithmetic.
  1470→  1470	
  1471→  1471	This is the **abstract pigeonhole principle**: more pigeons than
  1472→  1472	holes forces a collision. The counting argument replaces
  1473→  1473	case-by-case checking with a single size comparison.
  1474→  1474	"

  1010

  1025	World "Cardinality"
  1026	Level 15
  1027	
  1028	Title "Complement Counting"
  1029	
  1030	Introduction "
  1031	# Counting by Splitting
  1032	
  1033	Every finset `s` splits into two disjoint pieces relative to any
  1034	other finset `t`:
  1035	- The elements in both: $s \\cap t$
  1036	- The elements in `s` but not `t`: $s \\setminus t$
  1037	
  1038	Since these two pieces are disjoint and their union is `s`, their
  1039	cardinalities add up to $|s|$:
  1040	
  1041	$$|s \\setminus t| + |s \\cap t| = |s|$$
  1042	
  1043	In Lean:
  1044	```
  1045	Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t :
  1046	    (s \\ t).card + (s ∩ t).card = s.card
  1047	```
  1048	
  1049	This is **complement counting**: if you know two of the three
  1050	quantities ($|s|$, $|s \\setminus t|$, $|s \\cap t|$), you can compute
  1051	the third.
  1052	
  1053	**Your task**: Given $|s| = 8$ and $|s \\cap t| = 3$, compute
  1054	$|s \\setminus t|$.
  1055	"
  1056	
  1057	/-- Compute the set difference cardinality by complement counting. -/
  1058	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (hs : s.card = 8)
  1059	    (hi : (s ∩ t).card = 3) : (s \ t).card = 5 := by
  1060	  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` to get the
  1061	  splitting identity. Then use `omega` with the given sizes."
  1062	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try
  1063	  `have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` and then `omega`."
  1064	  have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t
  1065	  omega
  1066	
  1067	Conclusion "
  1068	Complement counting is the 'sibling' of inclusion-exclusion:
  1069	- **Inclusion-exclusion**: $|s \\cup t| + |s \\cap t| = |s| + |t|$
  1070	  — relates the union to the sum of sizes
  1071	- **Complement counting**: $|s \\setminus t| + |s \\cap t| = |s|$
  1072	  — relates the difference to the whole
  1073	
  1074	Both express the idea that counting should be additive on disjoint
  1075	pieces. In fact, complement counting IS a special case of disjoint
  1076	union: $s = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\cap t)$ is a disjoint
  1077	decomposition.
  1078	
  1079	In plain math, this is the strategy 'count the complement':
  1080	if you want $|s \\setminus t|$, compute $|s| - |s \\cap t|$.
  1081	"
  1082	
  1083	/-- `Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t` states that
  1084	`(s \\ t).card + (s ∩ t).card = s.card`.
  1085	
  1086	## Syntax
  1087	```
  1088	have h := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t
  1089	```
  1090	
  1091	## When to use it
  1092	When you need to relate `(s \\ t).card` to `s.card` and
  1093	`(s ∩ t).card`. This is complement counting: the set difference
  1094	and the intersection partition `s`.
  1095	
  1096	## Related
  1097	- `Finset.card_sdiff_of_subset h` — when `s ⊆ t`:
  1098	  `(t \\ s).card = t.card - s.card`
  1099	-/

  1111	Title "The Multiplication Principle"
  1112	
  1113	Introduction "
  1114	# Counting Pairs
  1115	
  1116	If you can choose one item from $s$ and one from $t$, how many
  1117	combined choices do you have? The answer is $|s| \\times |t|$.
  1118	
  1119	In Lean, `s ×ˢ t` is the **product** of two finsets — the set of
  1120	all pairs $(a, b)$ with $a \\in s$ and $b \\in t$:
  1121	
  1122	$$|s \\times^s t| = |s| \\cdot |t|$$
  1123	
  1124	```
  1125	Finset.card_product s t : (s ×ˢ t).card = s.card * t.card
  1126	```
  1127	
  1128	This is the **multiplication principle** — the second most important
  1129	counting identity after inclusion-exclusion.
  1130	
  1131	**Your task**: Given two finsets of known sizes, compute the size
  1132	of their product.
  1133	"
  1134	
  1135	/-- Compute the product cardinality from the individual sizes. -/
  1136	Statement (s t : Finset ℕ) (hs : s.card = 4) (ht : t.card = 3) :
  1137	    (s ×ˢ t).card = 12 := by
  1138	  Hint "Apply `Finset.card_product` to rewrite the left side,
  1139	  then substitute the known sizes."
  1140	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]`.
  1141	  This rewrites the goal step by step:
  1142	  `(s ×ˢ t).card` → `s.card * t.card` → `4 * t.card` → `4 * 3`."
  1143	  rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]
  1144	
  1145	Conclusion "
  1146	The multiplication principle: $|s \\times^s t| = |s| \\cdot |t|$.
  1147	
  1148	Unlike the additive card lemmas (`have` + `omega`), the product lemma
  1149	involves multiplication, so we use `rw` instead:
  1150	`rw [Finset.card_product, hs, ht]` directly computes the result.
  1151	
  1152	**Why 'product'?** The notation `s ×ˢ t` is the Cartesian product
  1153	specialized to finsets. Just as `Fin m × Fin n` has `m * n` elements
  1154	(which you could verify by exhaustive case analysis from Phase 1),
  1155	`s ×ˢ t` has `s.card * t.card` elements.
  1156	
  1157	The multiplication principle combines with inclusion-exclusion:
  1158	- **Addition**: $|A \\cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \\cap B|$ (for overlapping choices)
  1159	- **Multiplication**: $|A \\times B| = |A| \\cdot |B|$ (for independent choices)
  1160	
  1161	Together they handle the two fundamental counting scenarios:
  1162	'choose from A or B' vs. 'choose from A and B.'
  1163	"
  1164	
  1165	/-- `Finset.card_product s t` states that
  1166	`(s ×ˢ t).card = s.card * t.card`.
  1167	
  1168	This is the **multiplication principle**: the number of pairs
  1169	is the product of the individual counts.
  1170	
  1171	## Syntax
  1172	```
  1173	have h := Finset.card_product s t
  1174	```
  1175	
  1176	## When to use it
  1177	When the goal involves the cardinality of a product `s ×ˢ t`.
  1178	
  1179	## Notation
  1180	`s ×ˢ t` is typed as `\x\^s` or `\times\^s`.
  1181	-/

  1193	Title "The Universal Finset"
  1194	
  1195	Introduction "
  1196	# Finset.univ: All Elements of a Finite Type
  1197	
  1198	So far, your finsets have been built by hand: `insert`, `singleton`,
  1199	`range`, `filter`, `image`. But for a finite type like `Fin n`, there's
  1200	a finset that contains **all** elements: `Finset.univ`.
  1201	
  1202	`Finset.univ : Finset (Fin n)` — the finset of every element of `Fin n`.
  1203	
  1204	Two key facts connect `univ` to cardinality:
  1205	
  1206	- `Finset.card_univ : (Finset.univ : Finset α).card = Fintype.card α`
  1207	  — the size of `univ` equals the type's cardinality
  1208	- `Fintype.card_fin n : Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`
  1209	  — the type `Fin n` has exactly `n` elements
  1210	
  1211	Chaining these: `(Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 5)).card = 5`.
  1212	
  1213	**Your task**: Prove that `Finset.univ` for `Fin 5` has exactly 5 elements.
  1214	"
  1215	
  1216	/-- The universal finset of Fin 5 has 5 elements. -/
  1217	Statement : (Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 5)).card = 5 := by
  1218	  Hint "Use `Finset.card_univ` to convert the univ cardinality to
  1219	  `Fintype.card (Fin 5)`, then `Fintype.card_fin` to get `5`."
  1220	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin]`."
  1221	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin]
  1222	
  1223	Conclusion "
  1224	`Finset.univ` is the finset counterpart of 'the entire type.'
  1225	
  1226	| Tool | What it says |
  1227	|---|---|
  1228	| `Finset.univ` | The finset of all elements of a finite type |
  1229	| `Finset.card_univ` | `(Finset.univ).card = Fintype.card α` |
  1230	| `Fintype.card_fin n` | `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` |
  1231	
  1232	Together: `(Finset.univ : Finset (Fin n)).card = n`. This connects
  1233	the finset world (where you count elements of a set) to the type world
  1234	(where you count elements of a type).
  1235	
  1236	The next level introduces `Finset.subset_univ` — the fact that every
  1237	finset of a finite type is contained in `univ`. Combined with
  1238	`card_le_card`, this gives cardinality upper bounds.
  1239	"
  1240	
  1241	/-- `Finset.univ` is the finset of all elements of a finite type.
  1242	
  1243	For `Fin n`, `Finset.univ` contains all `n` elements.
  1244	
  1245	## Example
  1246	```
  1247	Finset.univ : Finset (Fin 3) -- contains 0, 1, 2
  1248	```
  1249	-/

  1274	World "Cardinality"
  1275	Level 18
  1276	
  1277	Title "Everything Lives in Univ"
  1278	
  1279	Introduction "
  1280	# Bounding Cardinality via Univ
  1281	
  1282	Every finset of a finite type is a subset of `Finset.univ`:
  1283	
  1284	```
  1285	Finset.subset_univ s : s ⊆ Finset.univ
  1286	```
  1287	
  1288	This is the finset version of 'every element belongs to the universe.'
  1289	
  1290	Combined with `card_le_card` (subsets have smaller cardinality), this
  1291	gives an upper bound on any finset's size:
  1292	
  1293	$$|s| \\leq |\\text{Fin}\\;n| = n$$
  1294	
  1295	The proof pattern:
  1296	1. `Finset.subset_univ s` gives `s ⊆ Finset.univ`
  1297	2. `Finset.card_le_card` converts subset to `s.card ≤ Finset.univ.card`
  1298	3. `Finset.card_univ` + `Fintype.card_fin` evaluates `Finset.univ.card`
  1299	
  1300	**Your task**: Prove that any finset of `Fin 4` elements has at most
  1301	4 elements.
  1302	"
  1303	
  1304	/-- Any finset of Fin 4 has at most 4 elements. -/
  1305	Statement (s : Finset (Fin 4)) : s.card ≤ 4 := by
  1306	  Hint "Use `Finset.subset_univ s` to get the subset relation,
  1307	  then `Finset.card_le_card` to convert it to a cardinality bound."
  1308	  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
  1309	  `have h1 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ s)`
  1310	  `rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1`
  1311	  `exact h1`"
  1312	  have h1 := Finset.card_le_card (Finset.subset_univ s)
  1313	  Hint "Now `h1 : s.card ≤ Finset.univ.card`. Simplify the right side
  1314	  using `card_univ` and `card_fin`."
  1315	  rw [Finset.card_univ, Fintype.card_fin] at h1
  1316	  Hint "Now `h1 : s.card ≤ 4`. This is exactly the goal."
  1317	  exact h1
  1318	
  1319	Conclusion "
  1320	You composed three tools:
  1321	1. `subset_univ` — every finset is a subset of the universe
  1322	2. `card_le_card` — subsets have smaller cardinality
  1323	3. `card_univ` + `card_fin` — the universe has `n` elements
  1324	
  1325	This gives the fundamental upper bound: no finset of `Fin n` elements
  1326	can have more than `n` elements. This may seem obvious, but it's the
  1327	key ingredient for the next level: proving the pigeonhole principle
  1328	via cardinality.
  1329	"
  1330	
  1331	/-- `Finset.subset_univ s` states that every finset `s` is a subset of `Finset.univ`.
  1332	
  1333	This is the finset version of 'every element belongs to the universe.'
  1334	-/
